[A43B 5/046] with the actuator being disposed at the lateral or medial side of the boot  A43B5/0464  takes precedence -- A43B 5/046 relates to sporting footwear in which an actuator-such as a switch, lever or electronic controller-is installed on the outer (lateral) or inner (medial) side of the boot. The subclass covers designs where the device is positioned on the side of the boot to operate adjustable or functional features.
[A43B 5/0462] with the actuator being disposed at the lower side of the boot  A43B5/0464  takes precedence -- This classification code, A43B 5/0462, pertains to sports footwear featuring an actuator mechanism located on the bottom side of the boot. It is part of a broader category that includes various characteristic features of footwear designed for sporting activities. However, it notes that a more specific code, A43B 5/0464, takes precedence when applicable.
[A43B 5/0464] with exchangeable parts -- A43B 5/0464 refers to sporting footwear that includes detachable components, such as interchangeable soles, cleats, uppers or other parts that can be removed and replaced to modify performance, fit or function. This subclass groups designs where the shoe's elements are meant to be swapped rather than being permanently fixed.
[A43B 5/0466] Adjustment of the side inclination of the boot leg; Canting -- This code covers mechanisms that allow the side angle of a boot's leg portion to be altered, commonly called canting, to improve alignment and performance for sports such as skiing or snowboarding. It pertains to adjustable side-inclination features integrated into the boot.
[A43B 5/0468] Adjustment of the angle of the boot to the ski -- A43B 5/0468 concerns mechanisms or features that allow the angle between a ski boot and the ski to be altered, enabling the skier to adjust stance or performance characteristics. It falls under the broader category of footwear designed for sporting purposes, specifically addressing adjustable boot-to-ski configurations. This classification covers devices such as hinges, swivels or adjustable bindings that modify the boot's tilt relative to the ski.
[A43B 5/047] provided with means to improve walking with the skiboot -- This subclass covers footwear designed for sporting use, specifically skiboots equipped with devices that assist or enhance walking, such as ankle support, traction aids, or mechanical aids. It falls under characteristic features of footwear in the human necessities hierarchy.
[A43B 5/0472] having a flexible toe portion; provided with a hinge at the ball of the foot -- This CPC subclass A43B 5/0472 pertains to sporting footwear that incorporates a flexible toe portion or a hinge located at the ball of the foot to improve toe movement during use. It is classified under characteristic features of footwear within the broader human necessities category.
[A43B 5/0474] having a walk/ski position  A43B5/0472  takes precedence -- A43B 5/0474 classifies sporting footwear that provides a walk-or ski position, such as shoes or boots designed to let the wearer adopt a stance suited for walking on snow or skiing. It is a subcategory of characteristic features of footwear and is superseded by the broader A43B 5/0472 subclass.
[A43B 5/0476] Front-entry skiboots -- Front-entry skiboots are ski boots that feature an opening at the front of the cuff, allowing the foot to be inserted without removing the boot's rear panel. This design facilitates easier on-and-off for the wearer while providing the support and insulation needed for skiing. The classification covers such boots intended for sporting use.
[A43B 5/0478] Mid-entry skiboots, hybrid skiboots -- A43B 5/0478 covers mid-entry and hybrid skiboot
[A43B 5/048] Rear-entry skiboots -- Rear-entry skiboots are ski boots designed with an opening at the back of the cuff that allows the foot to be inserted from behind rather than through a front or side entry. This configuration facilitates quick entry and removal, which is valuable for recreational skiers and racers who need to change boots frequently on the mountain. The design is classified under CPC A43B 5/048, a subgroup of footwear for sporting purposes.
[A43B 5/0482] made from materials with different rigidities -- This subclass covers sporting footwear whose construction combines components of varying stiffness, such as soles, midsoles or uppers made from materials with differing rigidity to provide performance benefits. It includes shoes where hard and flexible parts are integrated to influence cushioning, support or traction.
[A43B 5/0484] permitting easy replacement of parts -- A43B 5/0484 covers sporting footwear that incorporates design elements enabling the convenient replacement of components such as soles, insoles, cleats or other parts, allowing wearers to swap out worn or specialized elements without replacing the whole shoe.
[A43B 5/0486] characterized by the material -- A43B 5/0486 designates sporting footwear whose distinguishing feature is the material from which it is made, such as leather, textile, synthetic or composite constructions. The subclass falls under characteristic features of footwear for sporting purposes in the Human Necessities section.
[A43B 5/0488] with an upper made of flexible material, e.g. leather  A43B5/0401 ,  A43B5/002  take precedence -- A43B 5/0488 covers sporting footwear whose upper is made of a flexible material such as leather, falling under the characteristic features of footwear category. It specifies that the flexible upper is the defining element of the shoe's construction for athletic use.
[A43B 5/049] with an upper made of composite material, e.g. fibers or core embedded in a matrix -- A43B 5/049 refers to sporting footwear whose upper is made from composite materials, such as fibers or a reinforced core embedded in a matrix. It subclassifies designs that use these engineered constructions to provide strength, flexibility, or lightweight performance in athletic shoes.
[A43B 5/0492] Telemark boots -- Telemark boots are ski boots made for the Telemark style of skiing, featuring a flexible heel cuff that pivots independently from the sole to allow a heel-up motion, and a binding-compatible design that secures the boot to the ski. They typically combine a supportive upper of leather or synthetic material with a reinforced sole and may include adjustable straps or buckles for a snug fit.
[A43B 5/0494] Adaptations for soles or accessories associated with soles for telemark bindings -- This CPC subclass covers designs that modify a shoe's sole or add accessories to the sole to accommodate telemark ski bindings, allowing the heel to lift while the toe remains fixed for telemark skiing. It relates to sporting footwear adaptations.
[A43B 5/0496] boots for touring or hiking skis -- A43B 5/0496 covers boots designed specifically for ski touring or hiking on skis, a subcategory of sporting footwear. These boots combine features for mountaineering and ski performance, distinguishing them from ordinary ski boots and other hiking footwear.
[A43B 5/0498] For ski jumping -- A43B 5/0498 defines footwear specifically engineered for ski jumping, covering the shape, attachment system and performance-enhancing features that support take-off, aerial stability and safe landing in the sport. It falls under the hierarchy of human necessities, footwear and characteristic features of footwear for sporting purposes.
[A43B 5/06] Running shoes -- A43B 5/06 pertains to running shoes, a subclass of sporting footwear designed for athletes and recreational runners. These shoes provide lightweight construction, cushioning, and support features that enhance performance and help reduce the risk of injury.
[A43B 5/08] Bathing shoes -- A43B 5/08 covers bathing shoes, a type of footwear designed for use in water or on wet surfaces. These shoes typically have flexible, quick-dry uppers and slip-resistant soles that protect the foot while allowing easy movement during swimming, bathing, or other aquatic activities. They fall under the category of characteristic features of sporting footwear.
[A43B 5/10] Tennis shoes -- A43B 5/10 designates tennis shoes, a specific type of sporting footwear. It falls under the hierarchy of human necessities → footwear → characteristic features of footwear, and covers shoes intended for tennis and related athletic activities.
[A43B 5/12] Dancing shoes -- A43B 5/12 covers dancing shoes, a type of footwear designed for dance activities. It pertains to the characteristic features of sporting footwear, focusing on constructions that provide flexibility, support, and traction for dancers.
[A43B 5/14] Shoes for cyclists -- A43B 5/14 covers shoes specifically designed for cycling, featuring stiff soles, cleat attachment systems, and aerodynamic shapes to improve power transfer and
[A43B 5/145] Boots for motorcyclists -- A43B 5/145 covers boots specifically designed for motorcyclists, featuring protective elements such as reinforced toe and heel, ankle support, and materials resistant to abrasion and weather. These boots are grouped under characteristic features of footwear
[A43B 5/16] Skating boots -- A43B 5/16 covers skating boots, a type of footwear designed specifically for sporting activities such as ice skating or roller skating. The classification groups these boots under characteristic features of footwear within the broader human necessities and footwear categories.
[A43B 5/1608] size adjustable  skates with special foot-plates for the boot divided into two parts permitting adjustment to the size of the foot  A63C1/26 -- A43B 5/1608 pertains to size-adjustable skates featuring a boot divided into two sections, allowing for customization to fit different foot sizes. This classification is specific to skates designed with special foot-plates that enable adjustability, enhancing comfort and performance in sporting activities.
[A43B 5/1616] Inner boots -- Inner boots are removable or integral linings worn inside outer footwear for sporting activities, providing additional insulation, moisture control and impact protection. They are typically used in winter sports such as skiing or snowboarding, fitting within boots to keep the foot warm and cushioned while allowing the outer shell to retain its structural function.
[A43B 5/1625] made from materials with different rigidities -- Footwear for sport that incorporates components of differing rigidity, such as stiff shanks or reinforced plates alongside flexible uppers, to enhance support, stability and motion control. These constructions are used in activities where variable stiffness improves performance, like running, skiing or cycling.
[A43B 5/1633] Multipurpose skate boots  roller skates convertible to ice skates  A63C17/18 , with fixable wheels permitting walking  A63C17/20 -- A43B 5/1633 covers footwear that combines roller-skate and ice-skate functions, specifically skate boots convertible between the two modes and equipped with detachable wheels that allow the wearer to walk. This subclass falls under characteristic features of footwear for sporting purposes.
[A43B 5/1641] characterised by the sole  skates with special foot-plates for the boot  A63C1/22 ; characterised by the attachment of the skate  A43B5/1633  takes precedence; skates rigidly mounted to the sole of the boot  A63C1/02 -- A43B 5/1641 pertains to sports footwear, specifically ice or roller skates with unique foot-plates for the boot. This classification highlights features such as the skate's attachment method and the rigidity of its mounting to the sole, prioritizing these aspects over others in the design and functionality of the footwear.
[A43B 5/165] with ventilation means in the sole -- A43B 5/165 pertains to sporting footwear that includes ventilation means in the sole, such as channels, holes or porous materials, to enhance airflow and temperature regulation for the wearer.
[A43B 5/1658] provided with resilient means in the sole or between the chassis and the sole -- Footwear designed for sport that incorporates resilient elements such as air cells, gel pads or other cushioning structures placed in the sole itself or sandwiched between the chassis and the sole, providing shock absorption and increased comfort during activity.
[A43B 5/1666] characterised by the upper -- A43B 5/1666 covers sporting footwear whose distinguishing features relate to the upper, the part that covers the foot, including its construction, materials, shape or reinforcement. This subgroup falls under characteristic features of footwear for sporting purposes.
[A43B 5/1675] with ventilation means in the upper -- A43B 5/1675 covers sporting footwear that incorporates ventilation features in the upper part of the shoe, such as holes, mesh panels or other air-flow structures designed to improve breathability and comfort during activity.
[A43B 5/1683] characterised by the lower part of the upper or by the shell -- A43B 5/1683 classifies sporting footwear whose design is defined by the lower portion of the upper or by a shell element that forms part of the shoe. The feature may be a distinct material, shape or construction that distinguishes the lower upper or shell from the rest of the footwear. This subclass is used to identify shoes where that part is a principal functional or aesthetic characteristic.
[A43B 5/1691] characterised by the higher part of the upper, e.g. surrounding the ankle, by the quarter or cuff -- A43B 5/1691 designates sporting footwear whose defining element is the construction of the upper above the ankle, specifically the quarter or cuff surrounding the foot. This characteristic distinguishes the shoe's higher part of the upper, influencing fit, support, and performance for athletic use.
[A43B 5/18] Attachable overshoes for sporting purposes -- A43B 5/18 covers attachable overshoes designed for use in sports, such as removable spikes, cleats, or protective shells that can be fitted over regular shoes to improve traction or protect the foot during athletic activity.

[A43B 7/00] Footwear with health or hygienic arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Footwear with health (protection against injuries, walking aid for injured people, people with diseases) or hygienic arrangements.  Examples: ventilation systems for footwear, waterproof arrangements, support pads, resilient pads, located under strategic bones/regions of the foot.
[A43B 7/005] with cooling arrangements -- A43B 7/005 covers footwear that incorporates cooling features to enhance comfort or health, such as ventilation channels, moisture-wicking linings or built-in cooling packs. It belongs to the CPC group for characteristic features of footwear that provide health or hygienic benefits.
[A43B 7/02] with heating arrangements -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With electric heating arrangements -> A43B3/35
[A43B 7/06] ventilated -- Footwear classified under A43B 7/06 incorporates ventilation features designed to improve health or hygiene. These designs include openings, mesh panels, perforations or breathable materials that promote airflow and moisture management, helping to keep the feet cool, dry and less prone to odor or fungal growth. This subclass falls within the broader group of characteristic features of footwear aimed at health-related benefits.
[A43B 7/08] with air-holes, with or without closures -- A43B 7/08 covers footwear that includes health or hygienic features by providing air-holes for ventilation, with or without any type of closure. The openings improve foot comfort and moisture control, while the presence of closures is optional.
[A43B 7/081] the air being forced from outside -- A43B 7/081 classifies footwear that includes a mechanism to draw outside air into the shoe or boot for ventilation, providing health-oriented or hygienic benefits such as improved breathability and moisture control. This covers designs where airflow is actively forced from the external environment into the interior of the footwear.
[A43B 7/082] the air being expelled to the outside -- A43B 7/082 covers footwear designed with health or hygienic features that actively expel air to the outside, such as vents, perforations, or built-in fans that promote ventilation and moisture removal from the shoe interior. This subclass falls under characteristic features of footwear within the broader human necessities category.
[A43B 7/083] with pumping means or valves -- A43B 7/083 refers to footwear that incorporates a built-in pump, valve or similar pressure-regulating mechanism, allowing the shoe to be inflated, deflated or otherwise adjusted for health, therapeutic or hygienic purposes. The subclass groups designs where such devices are integral to the shoe's structure rather than added as separate accessories.
[A43B 7/084] characterised by the location of the holes -- A43B 7/084 covers footwear in which the placement of perforations, vents or other openings is a defining feature, such as holes positioned for breathability, drainage or therapeutic purposes. The classification is used for shoes, boots or sandals where the location of these holes is essential to the product's function or design.
[A43B 7/085] in the upper -- A43B 7/085 covers features incorporated into the upper part of footwear that provide health-related or hygienic benefits, such as materials, linings, or constructions designed to improve foot comfort, reduce odor, or protect against disease. This subclass falls under characteristic features of footwear aimed at enhancing wearer well-being.
[A43B 7/087] in the bottom of the sole -- The subclass A43B 7/087 covers footwear whose sole incorporates health-oriented or hygienic features, specifically designs applied to the bottom of the sole. It includes elements such as therapeutic, ergonomic, moisture-absorbing or antimicrobial components that improve the wearer's comfort or well-being.
[A43B 7/088] in the side of the sole -- The CPC code A43B 7/088 covers footwear in which a device or element is positioned on the side of the sole, such as a sidewall, ridge or insert that provides additional support, stability, or health-related benefits. This feature is a specific characteristic of shoes designed for hygienic or therapeutic purposes.
[A43B 7/10] with closable air-slots -- A43B 7/10 relates to footwear that includes air-slots which can be opened or closed to provide ventilation, promote foot health or improve hygiene, and may be part of therapeutic or comfort features.
[A43B 7/12] Special watertight footwear -- A43B 7/12 pertains to special watertight footwear, encompassing boots, shoes, galoshes and similar designs that are fully sealed to prevent water entry. The subclass covers constructions that use integrated seals, waterproof membranes or impermeable materials to protect the foot in wet or hazardous conditions.
[A43B 7/125] provided with a vapour permeable member, e.g. a membrane  A43B23/022  takes precedence -- This subclass concerns footwear that incorporates a vapour-permeable element, such as a membrane, to enhance breathability, hygiene or health-related performance. [A43B 7/14] with foot-supporting parts -- Footwear classified under A43B 7/14 includes designs that incorporate foot-supporting elements such as arches, pads, inserts or other structures intended to improve comfort, stability or health. These features may be built into the sole, insole, or upper and are intended to provide orthotic or ergonomic benefits while the shoes are worn.

[A43B 7/1415] characterised by the location under the foot -- A43B 7/1415 covers footwear where the functional or therapeutic element is placed beneath the foot, including insoles, footbeds, orthotic inserts or integrated cushioning layers designed to provide health, comfort or hygienic benefits.

[A43B 7/1463] with removable pads to allow custom fit -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Foot-supporting parts where a custom fit is provided for by at least one pad which can be removed or replaced with another pad. [A43B 7/1467] adjustable by resilient members, e.g. springs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Foot-supporting parts where at least one pad is adjustable through the elastic deformation of a resilient member.

[A43B 7/1485] Recesses or holes, traversing partially or completely the thickness of the pad -- A43B 7/1485 covers footwear designs that incorporate recesses or holes extending partially or fully through the thickness of the footpad, intended to provide health or hygienic benefits such as ventilation, pressure relief, or moisture management. [A43B 7/1495] with arch-supports of the bracelet type -- A43B 7/1495 covers footwear that incorporates arch-supports formed as a bracelet-type element encircling the foot or ankle, providing ergonomic or therapeutic support. [A43B 7/16] with elevated heel parts inside -- A43B 7/16 covers footwear that includes internal raised heel components designed for health or hygienic purposes, such as orthotic or therapeutic heels built into the shoe's structure. The classification applies to any shoe where the elevated heel part is situated inside the footwear rather than on the exterior.
[A43B 7/18] Joint supports, e.g. instep supports -- A43B 7/18 covers devices that are incorporated into footwear to support foot joints, such as instep braces or other joint-supporting elements. These items are intended to improve comfort, health or hygiene by providing structural reinforcement or corrective assistance while the shoe is worn.
[A43B 7/19] engaging the ground directly -- Footwear designed to promote health or hygiene by allowing the wearer's foot to make direct contact with the ground, often featuring minimal cushioning, thin soles, or specialized tread that mimics barefoot walking while still providing protection. This approach is intended to improve posture, balance, and natural foot movement.
[A43B 7/20] Ankle-joint supports or holders -- A43B 7/20 relates to footwear that incorporates health-oriented features specifically designed to support or hold the ankle joint. It covers integrated ankle-joint braces, stiffeners, straps or other devices that stabilize, protect or assist the ankle during use of the shoe.
[A43B 7/22] with fixed flat-foot insertions, metatarsal supports, ankle flaps or the like -- A43B 7/22 covers footwear that incorporates built-in health-oriented elements such as permanent flat-foot inserts, metatarsal supports, ankle flaps or similar structures, providing structural or hygienic benefits integrated directly into the shoe. This subgroup falls under characteristic features of footwear for human necessities.
[A43B 7/223] characterised by the constructive form -- This CPC subgroup covers footwear designed with health or hygienic purposes where the protective or therapeutic function is achieved through the shoe's constructive form rather than by added elements. It includes shoes whose shape, sole geometry or overall structure is specially fashioned to promote foot health, hygiene or comfort.
[A43B 7/226] characterised by the material -- A43B 7/226 in the Cooperative Patent Classification focuses on footwear with health or hygienic arrangements, specifically detailing characteristics related to the materials used. This classification is part of a broader hierarchy that addresses human necessities, particularly footwear and its distinctive features.
[A43B 7/24] Insertions or other supports preventing the foot canting to one side -- This CPC subgroup covers footwear that incorporates inserts or other support elements intended to keep the foot from tilting laterally. It includes devices such as medial or lateral wedges, arch supports or customized orthotic inserts integrated into shoes to prevent foot-canting.
[A43B 7/26] with toe-spacers or toe-spreaders -- A43B 7/26 classifies footwear that incorporates toe-spacers or toe-spreaders, designed to improve foot health or hygiene by keeping the toes separated. It falls under the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Footwear → Characteristic Features of Footwear.
[A43B 7/28] Adapting the inner sole of the shoe to the sole of the foot -- A43B 7/28 covers designs that modify a shoe's inner sole to fit the wearer's foot shape, providing health-related or hygienic benefits such as improved support, pressure distribution, or moisture management. This subclass falls under characteristic features of footwear aimed at enhancing foot comfort and well-being.
[A43B 7/30] Protecting the ball-joint against pressure while standing -- The CPC subclass A43B 7/30 covers footwear designed with features that protect the ball joint of the foot from pressure while standing, typically by incorporating cushioning, support structures, or ergonomic designs. It falls under characteristic features of footwear concerning health or hygienic arrangements.
[A43B 7/32] with shock-absorbing means -- A43B 7/32 covers footwear that incorporates shock-absorbing elements to reduce impact on the foot during walking, running or other activities. The classification includes shoes, boots, sandals or other footgear with integrated cushioning devices, springs or similar mechanisms designed to improve health or hygiene by mitigating shock.
[A43B 7/34] with protection against heat or cold -- A43B 7/34 covers footwear that incorporates measures to protect the wearer's feet from extreme temperatures, either by insulating against cold or shielding against heat. This includes shoes with thermal liners, heat-reflective or dissipating layers, built-in heating elements, or other designs that maintain a stable foot temperature in harsh environments.
[A43B 7/36] with earthing or grounding means -- Footwear classified under CPC A43B 7/36 includes designs that incorporate earthing or grounding features, such as conductive elements in the sole or stitching that connect the wearer to the earth. These health- or hygiene-oriented features aim to dissipate static electricity or improve physiological effects while walking.
[A43B 7/38] Elevating, i.e. height increasing -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With elevated heel parts inside -> A43B7/16 - Lengthening pieces for natural legs -> A61F3/00
[A43B 9/00] Footwear characterised by the assembling of the individual parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Assembling an upper to a sole, e.g. by stitching, gluing.
[A43B 9/02] Footwear stitched or nailed through -- A43B 9/02 covers footwear whose components are joined by stitching or nails, such as shoes where the upper is sewn or nailed to the sole. It includes manufacturing methods that secure parts by thread or metal fasteners rather than adhesive or molding.
[A43B 9/04] Welted footwear -- Welted footwear comprises shoes or boots whose upper and sole are joined by a welt - a strip of material that encircles the shoe and to which the outsole is attached - a construction method that provides enhanced durability, water resistance and ease of repair.
[A43B 9/06] stitched or nailed through -- A43B 9/06 covers footwear whose components are joined by stitching, nailing or similar mechanical fastening. The subclass includes shoes, boots and other foot coverings where the upper, sole or other parts are assembled through these methods rather than being molded or bonded, distinguishing them within the characteristic features of footwear.
[A43B 9/08] Turned footwear -- Turned footwear are shoes made by turning a single piece of material, such as leather, so that its outer surface becomes the interior and the seam forms the edge, creating a shoe without separate upper and sole components. This construction method is typical of slip-on and moccasin-type styles.
[A43B 9/10] Footwear with out-turned uppers -- Footwear with out-turned uppers are shoes whose upper part is folded or turned outward from the last, creating a visible edge or decorative rim around the shoe. This construction method is used for aesthetic or structural purposes and falls under CPC A43B 9/10, which covers footwear characterised by the assembling of individual parts.
[A43B 9/12] Stuck or cemented footwear -- Stuck or cemented footwear comprises shoes whose components-such as uppers, soles, heels or inserts-are joined together primarily by adhesives rather than stitching, molding or mechanical fasteners. This category includes any footwear assembled by gluing or cementing parts to form the final product.
[A43B 9/125] using a temporary protective insole or innersole -- A43B 9/125 covers footwear assembled from separate parts in which a temporary protective insole or inner sole is employed, such as removable pads that shield the foot or the shoe during manufacturing or wear.
[A43B 9/14] Platform shoes -- A43B 9/14 encompasses platform shoes, a type of footwear whose sole is thick-soled across its entire length to provide extra height and a distinctive look. The classification covers shoes made by assembling separate parts such as uppers, midsoles and the elevated sole into a single unit.
[A43B 9/16] Footwear with soles moulded on to uppers or welded on to uppers without adhesive -- A43B 9/16 covers footwear where the sole is formed directly onto the upper or welded to it without using adhesive. This includes shoes, boots and similar items whose sole and upper are joined by molding or welding processes, distinguishing them from conventional glued constructions.
[A43B 9/18] moulded -- Moulded footwear comprises shoes whose components are formed together in a single mould, creating a seamless or largely seamless structure. This manufacturing method typically uses synthetic or composite materials that are shaped and fused during the moulding process, eliminating the need for separate stitching or assembly of individual parts. It falls under the CPC class A43B 9/18, which covers footwear characterised by the assembling of the individual parts.
[A43B 9/20] welded -- A43B 9/20 covers footwear whose components are joined by welding rather than stitching or adhesives, including shoes, boots or other foot coverings where the upper, sole or other parts are fused together using heat or pressure.
[A43C] FASTENINGS OR ATTACHMENTS OF FOOTWEAR -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Equipment of the shoe e.g. laces, eyelets, cleats, studs.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Equipment for ski boots -> A43B5/0415 - Equipment for skate -> A43B5/16
[A43C 1/00] Shoe lacing fastenings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Every fastening system including at least one lace and concerning the lace, e.g. a special way of lacing, a specific position of the lace on the shoe, several laces used, etc.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Hooks for laces; Guards for hooks -> A43C3/00 - Eyelets -> A43C5/00 - Holding devices for laces -> A43C7/00 - Laces; Laces in general for garments made of textiles, leather, or plastics -> A43C9/00 - Cable fastening -> A43C11/16
[A43C 1/003] Zone lacing, i.e. whereby different zones of the footwear have different lacing tightening degrees, using one or a plurality of laces  laces combined with another system  A43C11/008 ; zone tightening using straps  A43C11/14 -- It refers to shoe-lacing arrangements in which distinct zones of a footwear item are tightened to different degrees, achieved with one or several laces, possibly combined with another fastening system, or by employing straps to provide zone-specific tightening.
[A43C 1/006] Rear lacing, i.e. with a lace placed on the back of the foot in place of, or in addition to the traditional front lace -- A43C 1/006 covers shoe fastening systems that employ rear lacing, where a lace is positioned on the back of the foot either as a substitute for or alongside the conventional front lacing, providing an alternative method of securing footwear.
[A43C 1/02] with elastic laces -- A43C 1/02 covers shoe lacing fastenings that incorporate elastic elements, allowing the
[A43C 1/04] with rings or loops -- A43C 1/04 covers shoe-lacing fastenings that employ rings or loops as the primary attachment elements. The classification applies to designs where the laces are threaded through circular or looped components to secure the footwear, distinguishing them from other lacing mechanisms such as hooks, eyelets or sliders. It falls under the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Footwear → Fastenings or Attachments of Footwear.
[A43C 1/06] tightened by draw-strings -- A43C 1/06 covers shoe-lacing fastenings that are tightened by draw-strings. It is classified under human necessities, footwear, fastenings or attachments of footwear, and specifically shoe lacing fastenings.
[A43C 11/00] Other fastenings specially adapted for shoes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any fastening that is not a lace. Still some of the documents can have a lace, in case they relate to footwear provided with more than one fastening system.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fastenings withdevice mounted on the tongue (could still involve lace) -> A43C11/20 - Ornaments and decorative devices with fastening function (could involve a lace) -> A43C11/24
[A43C 11/002] Fastenings using stretchable material attached to cuts in the uppers -- A43C 11/002 refers to footwear fastenings that use a stretchable material, such as elastic bands or woven cords, which are attached into cuts or openings in the shoe upper. These fastenings provide an adjustable, secure fit without relying on conventional laces, buckles or Velcro.
[A43C 11/004] Fastenings fixed along the upper edges of the uppers -- A43C 11/004 pertains to fastenings that are fixed along the upper edges of footwear uppers, such as sewn, glued or riveted strips that run the perimeter of the shoe. These permanent attachments reinforce the shoe's shape or add decorative detail and are not intended to be removable.
[A43C 11/006] Elastic fastenings -- A43C 11/006 relates to elastic fastenings used in footwear, such as stretchable bands, cords or straps that secure shoes by stretching and returning to their original shape. This subclass covers devices specifically designed to provide a flexible, adjustable closure for shoes and similar footwear.
[A43C 11/008] Combined fastenings, e.g. to accelerate undoing or fastening -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combination of two different fastenings e.g. a lace for precision lacing of a boot, and a zipper, for quick release of the boot.
[A43C 11/02] Button fastenings -- A43C 11/02 designates button fastenings used in footwear, covering various types of buttons that secure shoe components such as uppers, tongues or straps. These fastenings are specially adapted for shoes and fall under the category of other fastenings or attachments of footwear.
[A43C 11/04] Studs -- Studs are small protruding elements attached to the sole or upper of footwear to improve traction, protect against wear, or provide a decorative effect. In the CPC they fall under A43C 11/04, covering other fastenings specially adapted for shoes such as metal, plastic or rubber studs used on work boots, sports shoes and fashion footwear.
[A43C 11/06] Snap-button fastenings -- A43C 11/06 designates snap-button fastenings used on shoes, covering devices that latch together by a spring-loaded button mechanism. It falls under the footwear section of the CPC, specifically the subclass for other fastenings specially adapted for shoes.
[A43C 11/08] Hook fastenings -- A43C 11/08 covers hook fastenings that are specially adapted for shoes, falling under the footwear fastenings subclass of the human necessities section. This entry classifies any hook-type closure used to secure or adjust footwear.
[A43C 11/10] Hooks with several tongues -- A43C 11/10 covers hooks that have multiple tongues used as fasteners on shoes and other footwear. These components enable a secure, adjustable attachment of uppers to soles or straps.
[A43C 11/12] Slide or glide fastenings -- Slide or glide fastenings are footwear attachment mechanisms that allow the shoe to
[A43C 11/14] Clamp fastenings -- A43C 11/14 designates clamp-type fastenings used on shoes, covering any fastening device that grips the shoe upper or sole by clamping action rather than laces, Velcro or buckles. It falls under the footwear fastenings subgroup of the CPC hierarchy.
[A43C 11/1406] Fastenings with toggle levers; Equipment therefor -- A43C 11/1406 covers fastenings that use toggle levers, such as snap-type or lever-actuated closures, and the associated mechanisms or tools required to install, adjust, or release them on footwear. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for human necessities, footwear, and other fastenings specially adapted for shoes.
[A43C 11/1413] Equipment for fastening toggle lever fastenings -- Equipment designed to secure toggle-lever fastenings on shoes, such as levers or snaps that lock the footwear in place. It falls under other fastenings specially adapted for shoes within the footwear fastenings hierarchy. This includes tools, devices or mechanisms that assist in applying, adjusting or releasing the toggle lever components.
[A43C 11/142] Fastenings with toggle levers with adjustment means provided for on the shoe, e.g. rack -- This subclass covers shoe fastenings that use toggle levers equipped with an adjustment mechanism, such as a rack, enabling the fit to be altered. It falls under fastenings or attachments of footwear within the Human Necessities section.
[A43C 11/1426] characterised by the attachment of the rack to the footwear -- The A43C 11/1426 subclass covers footwear that includes a rack directly attached to the shoe, serving as an integrated support or storage element. It lies within the CPC hierarchy for human necessities, specifically fastenings or attachments of footwear, and addresses other fastenings specially adapted for shoes.
[A43C 11/1433] characterised by means to decrease required force for the closure movement of the toggle lever -- A43C 11/1433 classifies shoe fastenings that include mechanisms-such as springs, levers or cams-designed to lower the force needed to close a toggle lever, making the closure movement easier for the wearer.
[A43C 11/144] characterised by means to retain the preferred position on the rack when the fastening means are in the loosened position -- A43C 11/144 covers shoe fastenings specially adapted to keep footwear in its intended rack position even when the closure is loosened, ensuring the shoe remains stable during storage or display. This falls under Human Necessities > Footwear > Fastenings or Attachments of Footwear, and pertains to other fastenings designed specifically for shoes.
[A43C 11/1446] characterised by special protection means to prevent damage or accidental loosening of the fastening means -- A43C 11/1446 covers shoe fastenings that incorporate protective features intended to avoid damage or accidental loosening of the closure. It includes designs such as reinforced tabs, locking devices or shielding elements that secure the fastening in use. This subclass falls under other fastenings specially adapted for footwear.
[A43C 11/1453] characterised by the shape of the teeth on the rack -- A43C 11/1453 concerns footwear fastenings that are distinguished by the shape of the teeth on a rack or gear element used to secure the shoe. The subclass groups designs where the tooth profile of the rack is a defining functional or aesthetic feature of the attachment.
[A43C 11/146] Fastenings with toggle levers with adjustment means provided for on the strap, e.g. ratchet strap -- A43C 11/146 pertains to footwear fastenings that use toggle levers with an integrated adjustment mechanism, such as ratchet straps, enabling precise tightening of the strap. This sub-class falls under other fastenings specially adapted for shoes within the Human Necessities-Footwear hierarchy.
[A43C 11/1466] characterised by means to decrease required force for the closure movement of the toggle lever -- A43C 11/1466 falls under the footwear section of the Cooperative Patent Classification, specifically the subclass for other fastenings specially adapted for shoes. It covers shoe-closure mechanisms that incorporate features to reduce the force needed to operate a toggle-lever closure, improving ease of fastening.
[A43C 11/1473] characterised by means to retain the preferred position on the strap when the fastening means are in the loosened position -- This CPC subclass defines shoe fastenings that keep a strap in its intended location even when the closure is loosened, using devices such as clips, hooks or similar means to prevent the strap from shifting during adjustment. It is indexed as A43C 11/1473 within the footwear fastenings hierarchy.
[A43C 11/148] characterised by special protection means to prevent damage or accidental loosening of the fastening means -- A43C 11/148 covers footwear fastenings that incorporate special protective features intended to avoid damage and prevent the fastening from becoming unintentionally loose.
[A43C 11/1486] characterised by the shape of the teeth on the ratchet strap -- A43C 11/1486 pertains to shoe fastenings in which the ratchet strap is distinguished by the specific shape of its teeth. The design of the teeth governs the engagement mechanism, allowing adjustable, secure attachment of the strap to the footwear.
[A43C 11/1493] Strap fastenings having hook and loop-type fastening elements -- A43C 11/1493 covers strap fastenings for footwear that employ hook-and-loop (Velcro-type) elements, where one side of the strap has tiny hooks that engage with looped fabric on the opposite side to secure the shoe. It includes designs in which the fastener is built into the strap itself or attached to the shoe surface.
[A43C 11/16] Fastenings secured by wire, bolts, or the like -- A43C 11/16 designates fastenings for shoes that are secured by wire, bolts or similar mechanical devices. It belongs to the CPC hierarchy Human Necessities - Footwear - Fastenings or Attachments of Footwear - Other fastenings specially adapted for shoes.
[A43C 11/165] characterised by a spool, reel or pulley for winding up cables, laces or straps by rotation -- A43C 11/165 covers footwear fastenings that incorporate a spool, reel or pulley mechanism to wind up cables, laces or straps by rotation. These devices are designed to tighten or secure shoes automatically, often found in specialized or adaptive footwear. It falls under the broader category of other fastenings specially adapted for shoes.
[A43C 11/18] Fastenings of the lazy-tongs type -- A43C 11/18 classifies shoe fastenings of the lazy-tongs type, meaning devices that use a hinged or spring-loaded mechanism to grip the shoe's upper and keep it closed while allowing easy insertion of the foot. These fasteners are treated as "other fastenings specially adapted for shoes" within the broader footwear fastening categories of the CPC.
[A43C 11/20] Fastenings with tightening devices mounted on the tongue -- A43C 11/20 covers fastenings for footwear that use a tightening device mounted on the tongue, such as laces, straps or buck
[A43C 11/22] Fastening devices with elastic tightening parts between pairs of eyelets, e.g. clamps, springs, bands -- A43C 11/22 covers fastening devices that use elastic tightening elements placed between pairs of eyelets on shoes, such as clamps, springs or bands. It falls under the hierarchy Human Necessities → Footwear → Fastenings or Attachments of Footwear → Other fastenings specially adapted for shoes.
[A43C 11/24] Ornamental buckles or other ornaments for shoes, with fastening function -- A43C 11/24 covers ornamental buckles and other decorative fittings that also serve to fasten shoes. These components are designed primarily for aesthetic appeal while providing a functional fastening mechanism on footwear.
[A43C 13/00] Wear-resisting attachments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Additional device(s) attached on the shoe to protect against wear.
[A43C 13/02] Metal plates for soles or heels -- This CPC subclass A43C 13/02 covers metal plates that are attached to the soles or heels of shoes to resist wear, serving as reinforcement or protective plates. These components are mounted by adhesives, stitching or mechanical fasteners to increase the durability of footwear.
[A43C 13/04] Cleats -- A43C 13/04 pertains to cleats, which are wear-resisting fastenings attached to footwear to improve grip on soft or slippery surfaces. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Footwear → Fastenings or Attachments of Footwear.
[A43C 13/06] Attachments for edges of soles, especially for ski boots -- Attachments for the edges of shoe soles that improve wear resistance, especially devices fitted to ski-boot soles to protect the peripheral area and enhance durability. These fasteners are designed to resist abrasion and maintain the shape of the sole during use.
[A43C 13/08] with rubber, plastics, leather, felt or like parts -- A43C 13/08 covers fastenings or attachments on footwear that incorporate rubber, plastic, leather, felt or similar materials to resist wear. These components are designed to enhance durability and provide secure closure for shoes, boots or similar footwear. It falls under the Human Necessities > Footwear > Fastenings or Attachments of Footwear hierarchy.
[A43C 13/10] made from wire -- A43C 13/10 refers to foot-wear fastenings or attachments that are constructed from wire and are intended to resist wear, such as wire-based clasps, hooks or eyelets used to secure shoes. This subclass falls under Human Necessities → Footwear → Fastenings or Attachments of Footwear.
[A43C 13/12] Releasable protecting-soles of metal -- A43C 13/12 covers metal protective soles that can be detached from footwear. These releasable metal inserts are used to shield the shoe's sole from wear and are designed to be removed and replaced as needed.
[A43C 13/14] Special attachments for toe-caps -- A43C 13/14 covers specialized fasteners or fittings designed specifically for toe-caps on footwear, such as protective reinforcement, decorative elements or mechanisms that secure the toe-cap to the shoe. The code falls under Human Necessities → Footwear → Fastenings or Attachments of Footwear, focusing on wear-resisting attachments for toe-caps.
[A43C 15/00] Non-skid devices or attachments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Additional device(s) attached to a footwear to improve friction.
[A43C 15/005] Nails, pins -- A43C 15/005 relates to nails and pins employed as non-skid devices or attachments on footwear, such as metal spikes or pins that improve traction or help secure the sole to the upper.
[A43C 15/02] attached to the sole -- A43C 15/02 designates non-skid devices or attachments that are fixed to the sole of footwear. It covers elements such as cleats, studs, tread patterns or other traction-enhancing features that are permanently attached to the shoe's lower surface.
[A43C 15/04] attached to the heel -- A43C 15/04 designates non-skid devices or other attachments that are fixed to the heel of footwear. This subclass covers elements such as heel-mounted cleats, spikes, rubber pads or similar structures intended to increase traction or provide functional attachment at the heel region of shoes.
[A43C 15/06] Ice-gripping devices or attachments, e.g. ice-spurs, ice-cleats, ice-creepers, crampons -- A43C 15/06 covers devices or attachments that improve traction on icy surfaces when fitted to footwear, such as ice-spurs, ice-cleats, ice-creepers and crampons, providing non-skid performance for the wearer.
[A43C 15/061] Ice-gripping devices or attachments, e.g. ice-cleats, ice-creepers -- A43C 15/061 relates to non-skid devices that are mounted on footwear to improve traction on icy surfaces, such as detachable ice cleats or ice-creepers that engage the ground to prevent slipping. These attachments are classified under footwear fastenings in the CPC system.
[A43C 15/063] with ice-gripping means projecting from the front foot region -- This subgroup pertains to footwear equipped with ice-gripping elements that protrude from the front foot area, such as studs, spikes or similar projections designed to improve traction on icy surfaces. It falls under non-skid devices or attachments for shoes.
[A43C 15/065] with ice-gripping means projecting from the region directly in front of the heel -- A43C 15/065 covers non-slip attachments for footwear that provide ice-gripping elements extending from the area immediately ahead of the heel. These devices are designed to improve traction on icy surfaces by projecting spikes, studs, or similar protrusions directly in front of the heel region.
[A43C 15/066] with ice-gripping means projecting from the heel area, e.g. ice spurs -- A43C 15/066 pertains to non-skid attachments for footwear that feature ice-gripping elements extending from the heel area, such as detachable or integral ice spurs. These devices are intended to enhance traction on frozen surfaces by projecting studs or spikes forward from the heel, and are commonly applied to boots used in icy or snowy conditions.
[A43C 15/068] Climbing devices or attachments, e.g. glacier crampons, mountain climbing irons -- A43C 15/068 covers devices that can be fastened to footwear to improve grip on steep or icy surfaces, such as glacier crampons and mountain-climbing irons. These non-skid attachments are designed to secure the foot and provide traction during climbing activities, fitting within the broader category of fastenings or attachments for footwear.
[A43C 15/08] Reversible ice-spikes -- Reversible ice-spikes are detachable traction devices fitted to the sole of footwear to improve grip on icy surfaces; the spikes can be flipped so the smooth side faces outward when traction is not needed. They are classified as non-skid attachments for shoes.
[A43C 15/09] Equipment associated with footwear for walking on inclines to compensate for angle of inclination -- A43C 15/09 refers to footwear attachments that help a wearer keep a level foot position when walking on slopes, such as built-in wedges, cleats or adjustable heel lifts designed to compensate for the angle of inclination.
[A43C 15/10] Non-skid attachments made of wire, chain, or other meshed material -- The code A43C 15/10 refers to non-skid devices or attachments that are fixed to footwear and are made from wire, chain or other meshed materials, intended to improve traction and prevent slipping. These elements belong to the footwear fastenings group within the broader human necessities category.
[A43C 15/12] Small releasable non-slip straps -- A43C 15/12 covers small releasable non-slip straps designed as attachments for footwear to improve traction. These devices are typically short, adjustable straps that can be fastened or released as needed, providing a quick means to prevent slipping on smooth surfaces. They fall under the CPC subgroup for non-skid devices or attachments of shoes.
[A43C 15/14] with outwardly-movable spikes -- A43C 15/14 covers non-skid devices or attachments for footwear that feature outwardly-movable spikes, such as cleats that can be raised, pivoted or otherwise displaced from the sole to improve traction on slippery surfaces.
[A43C 15/16] Studs for football or like boots -- A43C 15/16 covers studs incorporated in football or similar boots to provide traction on grassy or synthetic surfaces. The subclass includes designs, arrangements and mounting of metal or polymer studs that function as non-slip devices attached to the sole of the footwear.
[A43C 15/161] characterised by the attachment to the sole -- A43C 15/161 pertains to non-skid devices or attachments that are fixed to the sole of footwear. It includes elements such as rubber pads, protruding studs, textured patterns or other traction-enhancing features that are integral or added onto the sole to improve grip on surfaces.
[A43C 15/162] characterised by the shape -- A43C 15/162 relates to non-skid devices or attachments for footwear whose anti-slip function is achieved primarily by their geometric shape, such as tread patterns, studs or protrusions formed on soles or heels.
[A43C 15/164] having a circular cross section -- A43C 15/164 pertains to non-skid devices or attachments for footwear that are shaped with a circular cross-section, such as round studs, pads or treads designed to improve traction. It falls under Human Necessities → Footwear → Fastenings or Attachments of Footwear → Non-skid devices or attachments.
[A43C 15/165] pointed or conical, e.g. calks, spikes, pins -- This subclass covers footwear attachments that are shaped as points or cones, such as calks, spikes or pins, intended to increase traction on slippery or uneven surfaces. These elements are classified as non-skid devices for shoes.
[A43C 15/167] frusto-conical or cylindrical -- A43C 15/167 covers non-skid devices or attachments for footwear that are shaped as frusto-conical or cylindrical elements, such as cones, cylinders or tapered studs applied to soles or uppers to improve traction on slippery surfaces.
[A43C 15/168] with resilient means, e.g. shock absorbing means -- A43C 15/168 refers to non-skid devices or attachments for footwear that incorporate resilient or shock-absorbing means, such as cushioning elements built into fastenings or sole sections to reduce impact and improve comfort. It covers any attachment that combines anti-slip features with a spring-like or shock-mitigating component.
[A43C 15/18] Serrated grips -- A43C 15/18 covers serrated grips, which are non-skid devices attached to footwear to improve traction on slippery surfaces. These serrated elements, often made of rubber or metal, are mounted on soles or heels
[A43C 17/00] Spurs -- Spurs are metal or plastic devices attached to the heel of a shoe or boot to provide a sharp point or projection for riding or agricultural use. In the Cooperative Patent Classification they fall under A43C 17/00, which groups fastenings or attachments of footwear that are designed to hold such ancillary elements. This subclass is part of the broader hierarchy of HUMAN NECESSITIES → FOOTWEAR → FASTENINGS OR ATTACHMENTS OF FOOTWEAR.
[A43C 17/02] Spurs -- Spurs are detachable fittings attached to the heel of a boot, typically used by riders to cue a horse through a sharp point or a small rowel. In the classification they fall under footwear fastenings, covering designs, mounting mechanisms and variations of the spurs' shape, material and attachment method.
[A43C 17/04] Releasable spur fastenings -- A43C 17/04 covers fastening devices that enable a spur to be attached to footwear but can be released without removing the shoe. The classification includes detachable brackets, clips or levers designed for mounting spurs on boots, allowing quick removal for storage or when the spur is not needed.
[A43C 17/06] Collapsible spurs -- Collapsible spurs are detachable or fold-away devices mounted on the heel of riding boots that can be extended for use and folded or removed for storage or walking. They provide a convenient, adjustable means of applying pressure to a horse's sides while minimizing bulk when not in use.
[A43C 19/00] Attachments for footwear, not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any attachment to a footwear not covered by the other groups, e.g. a plate with the name of the user, a tool to clean a golf club with the foot, an electronic device, any attachment not integrated in the footwear.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Overshoes -> A43B3/16 - Overshoes for sporting purpose -> A43B5/18
[A43C 3/00] Hooks for laces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hooks for lacing, along the upper, generally the lace joining together the hooks in the lateral part with the hooks situated on the medial part of the upper.
[A43C 3/02] Lacing-hooks with guide rollers -- Lacing-hooks with guide rollers are fastening devices for shoes that incorporate small rollers to guide the lace smoothly through the hook, reducing friction and wear. They belong to the subclass of footwear attachments that use hooks to secure laces, classified under A43C 3/02.
[A43C 3/04] Spring safety-hooks -- A43C 3/04 covers spring safety-hooks, a type of fastening used on shoes to secure laces quickly and reliably. These hooks employ a spring mechanism that grips the lace when pulled and releases when pressed, providing an easy-to-use alternative to traditional eyelets or knots. They are classified under footwear fastenings within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[A43C 5/00] Eyelets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Holes in the upper generally to help the lace to bring together the lateral part with the medial part of the upper
[A43C 7/00] Holding-devices for laces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems to hold lace(s) in tighten position.
[A43C 7/005] the devices having means to hold the traditional knots or part of it tightened -- A43C 7/005 relates to devices that secure shoe laces by holding the traditional knot or a portion of it in a tightened position, such as lace locks, aglets, or specialized eyelet mechanisms designed to keep the knot from loosening during use.
[A43C 7/02] Flaps -- A43C 7/02 covers flaps that serve as holding devices for shoe laces, a subcategory of fastening mechanisms on footwear. The code is nested under Human Necessities → Footwear → Fastenings or Attachments of Footwear and pertains specifically to flap designs that secure or adjust laces.
[A43C 7/04] Hinged devices -- A43C 7/04 covers hinged devices that serve as holding-devices for shoe laces. These mechanisms use a pivoting element to secure or release the lace, allowing easy fastening and adjustment of footwear.
[A43C 7/06] Elastic bands -- A43C 7/06 pertains to elastic bands that serve as holding-devices for shoe laces, a type of fastening or attachment used in footwear. The class is nested under Human Necessities → Footwear → Fastenings or Attachments of Footwear.
[A43C 7/08] Clamps drawn tight by laces -- A43C 7/08 covers devices that clamp or hold parts of footwear in place by means of laces drawn tight, such as lace-tightening clamps or locking mechanisms integrated into shoes to secure them while the laces are tightened. This falls under fastenings or attachments of footwear within the human necessities hierarchy.
[A43C 9/00] Laces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The lace itself.
[A43C 9/02] provided with tags, buttons, or decorative tufts -- A43C 9/02 refers to footwear that incorporates decorative attachments such as tags, buttons or ornamental tufts, either as part of the design or for functional purposes. It classifies shoes where these embellishments are added to the upper or other components to enhance appearance or provide specific fastening or identification features.
[A43C 9/04] Forming ends of laces of plastics, celluloid, rubber, or the like -- A43C 9/04 covers methods and apparatus for shaping the terminal portions of shoelace ends when the laces are made from plastics, celluloid, rubber or similar synthetic materials. The classification addresses the formation of aglets, tips or other finishing features that improve durability, ease of threading and aesthetic appearance of the laces.
[A43C 9/06] Releasable ends of laces -- A43C 9/06 covers footwear laces whose ends can be detached or released, such as snap-type or magnetic tips that allow the lace to be removed without untying. The feature is intended to simplify fastening and unfastening while maintaining secure attachment during use.
[A43C 9/08] made of paper or wire -- A43C 9/08 designates laces for footwear whose material is paper or wire, covering any fastening or attachment of shoes that uses these non-textile, non-synthetic strands. This subclass falls under the hierarchy HUMAN NECESSITIES → FOOTWEAR → FASTENINGS OR ATTACHMENTS OF FOOTWEAR.
[A43D] MACHINES, TOOLS, EQUIPMENT OR METHODS FOR MANUFACTURING OR REPAIRING FOOTWEAR -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All the machines and tools to build a shoe. Each group relates to a machine, or a step of the production. Most of them are explicit. A more detailed explanation will be given to the others.
[A43D 1/00] Measuring devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Essentially (but not necessarily) in order to build a model in order to produce a shoe.
[A43D 100/00] Setting or removing eyelets, buttons, lacing-hooks, or elastic gussets in shoes -- A43D 100/00 relates to methods and apparatus for setting or removing eyelets, buttons, lacing-hooks or elastic gussets in shoes, covering both manufacturing and repair operations within the footwear sector. It falls under the broader categories of human necessities, footwear and equipment for making or fixing shoes.
[A43D 100/02] Punching and eyelet-setting machines or tools -- A43D 100/02 classifies punching and eyelet-setting machines or tools used in footwear production, specifically equipment that sets or removes eyelets, buttons, lacing-hooks or elastic gussets in shoes. This falls under the broader categories of human necessities, footwear, and manufacturing or repairing footwear machinery.
[A43D 100/04] inserting invisible eyelets -- The CPC subclass A43D 100/04 covers the process of inserting invisible eyelets in shoes, a specific method used when setting or removing eyelets, buttons, lacing-hooks or elastic gussets during footwear manufacture or repair.
[A43D 100/06] inserting two eyelets simultaneously -- A43D 100/06 covers a method for simultaneously installing a pair of eyelets in a shoe or other footwear during production. The technique involves setting two eyelets at once, improving efficiency in the manufacturing process for lacing components.
[A43D 100/08] Setting buttons on footwear -- A43D 100/08 pertains to methods and devices for attaching buttons, eyelets, lacing-hooks or elastic gussets to shoes, essentially covering the setting of buttons on footwear. It falls within the cooperative patent classification for machines, tools, equipment or processes used in manufacturing or repairing footwear, which is a subdivision of the Human Necessities category.
[A43D 100/10] Machines or tools for setting lacing-hooks in shoes -- A43D 100/10 covers machines and tools specifically designed to install lacing-hooks in footwear, a subcategory of equipment for setting eyelets, buttons, lacing-hooks or elastic gussets. It belongs to the CPC hierarchy Human Necessities - Footwear - Machines, tools, equipment or methods for manufacturing or repairing footwear.
[A43D 100/12] Machines for inserting elastic gussets in shoes -- A43D 100/12 covers machines that insert elastic gussets into shoes during manufacturing. These devices automate the placement of stretchable inserts to improve fit and comfort in footwear production.
[A43D 100/14] Devices for removing buttons, lacing-hooks, or the like from shoes -- The subclass includes tools specifically made to detach fastening parts such as buttons, lacing-hooks, eyelets or elastic gussets from shoes, facilitating repair or manufacturing operations on footwear.
[A43D 1/02] Foot-measuring devices -- A43D 1/02 covers devices used to measure the dimensions of a human foot, such as foot length, width, arch height and volume, to aid in the design, fitting or manufacturing of footwear. The class includes manual gauges, digital scanners, pressure mats and other instruments that capture foot shape or size data.
[A43D 1/022] involving making footprints or permanent moulds of the foot -- A43D 1/022 relates to devices or methods that record the shape of a foot, such as taking foot impressions or creating permanent foot molds, primarily for use in designing, fitting or repairing footwear and orthotic products.
[A43D 1/025] comprising optical means, e.g. mirrors, photo-electric cells, for measuring or inspecting feet -- A43D 1/025 refers to footwear-related measuring devices that employ optical components such as mirrors or photo-electric cells to capture foot dimensions or shape. These systems use light-based methods to inspect or assess the foot for purposes like fitting, quality control or custom shoe production.
[A43D 1/027] Shoe fit indicating devices -- Devices that indicate whether a shoe properly fits the foot, such as fitting gauges, pressure or deformation sensors, and visual or audible indicators that compare shoe dimensions to a foot model. They are used in production or repair to assess fit accuracy and ensure comfort and size conformity.
[A43D 1/04] Last-measuring devices -- A43D 1/04 covers devices used to measure the dimensions of a shoe-last, the form that determines the shape of footwear. These instruments help manufacturers and repairers ensure proper fit and consistency when making or adjusting shoes.
[A43D 1/06] Measuring devices for the inside measure of shoes, for the height of heels, or for the arrangement of heels -- A43D 1/06 covers devices used to measure the internal dimensions of footwear, the height of heels, or to assist in positioning heels during manufacturing or fitting
[A43D 1/08] Measuring devices for shoe parts -- A43D 1/08 pertains to measuring devices employed in the manufacture or repair of footwear, specifically tools that gauge dimensions of shoe parts such as soles, uppers and components. These instruments enable manufacturers to verify size, shape and fit, supporting quality control and precise production.
[A43D 11/00] Machines for preliminary treatment or assembling of upper-parts, counters, or insoles on their lasts preparatory to the pulling-over or lasting operations -- A43D 11/00 covers machines used in footwear production that perform preliminary treatment or assembly of upper-parts, counters or insoles on lasts before the pulling-over or lasting stage. These devices prepare the components for the final shaping and attachment to the shoe sole.
[A43D 11/003] Applying or removing protective coverings -- A43D 11/003 covers machines or methods used to apply or strip protective coverings on footwear components during preparatory steps, such as adhesives, tapes, films or sleeves that shield uppers, counters or insoles before the pulling-over or lasting operations. It is part of equipment for preliminary treatment or assembling of footwear parts.
[A43D 11/006] Devices for temporarily fixing or aligning insoles on lasts -- Machines that hold, fix or align insoles on lasts during the early stages of shoe assembly, allowing temporary positioning before pulling-over or lasting processes. These devices are used to prepare the insole on the last for subsequent shaping, stitching or attachment steps.
[A43D 11/01] Machines for applying reinforcement or ornamental straps to the margins of uppers -- Machines that attach reinforcement or decorative straps to the edges of shoe uppers during manufacturing, typically before the lasting stage. They feed, position and affix the straps by stitching, gluing or pressing, improving durability or aesthetic appearance.
[A43D 11/02] to the margins thereof -- A43D 11/02 covers machines that treat or assemble the margins of lasts during the preliminary preparation of shoe uppers, counters or insoles before pulling-over or lasting operations. The classification is applied to devices that shape, trim or otherwise process the edges of the last to improve fit and manufacturing efficiency.
[A43D 11/03] Coating with thermoplastic materials to stiffen the toe or heel portion -- A43D 11/03 covers processes that stiffen the toe or heel of footwear by applying a thermoplastic coating. The method involves depositing, heating or curing the thermoplastic on the selected region of the shoe upper or sole during manufacture. It is classified under machines for preliminary treatment or assembling of upper-parts, counters or insoles before the pulling-over or lasting operations.
[A43D 11/04] Machines for seam-pressing or flattening shoe parts, quarters, or the like -- A43D 11/04 covers machines that press or flatten seams, quarters and similar shoe components during manufacturing. The equipment is used after uppers are assembled on lasts, applying pressure to smooth and align parts before the final lasting or pulling-over step, improving fit and surface quality.
[A43D 11/06] Machines for temporary buttoning of shoe parts -- A43D 11/06 covers machines that temporarily button shoe components such as uppers, counters or insoles to a last during early assembly stages. These devices prepare the parts for subsequent pulling-over or lasting operations in footwear manufacturing.
[A43D 11/08] Machines for temporary lacing of shoe parts or for cutting-away the lacing strips after lasting -- The A43D 11/08 class covers equipment used in shoe manufacturing that temporarily fastens upper components during the assembling or lasting stage, and devices that remove those temporary laces after the shoe is shaped on its last. These machines facilitate the handling of uppers, counters or insoles before the final pulling-over or lasting operation.
[A43D 11/085] Lacing machines -- A43D 11/085 covers machines that automatically fasten or sew laces onto shoe uppers during production, performing the lacing step before the lasting operation. These lacing machines are used in footwear manufacturing to streamline the assembly of the upper-part and prepare the shoe for final shaping.
[A43D 11/10] Devices for holding the lacing portions in position during lasting -- A43D 11/10 relates to devices that secure the lacing portions of a shoe upper while the shoe is being lasted on a form. These tools hold the laces in the correct position and tension during the assembly of uppers, counters or insoles onto the last.
[A43D 111/00] Miscellaneous mechanisms for shoemaking -- A43D 111/00 covers a range of auxiliary devices, tools and methods employed in the production of shoes that are not classified elsewhere in the footwear manufacturing group. It includes assorted mechanisms such as automatic feeding, stitching aids, shaping or finishing apparatuses and other equipment that support shoemaking operations but do not fit into more specific sub-categories.
[A43D 111/003] with clamping or gripping mechanism -- This CPC subclass covers shoe-making machines that employ a clamping or gripping mechanism to hold a workpiece or shoe during manufacturing steps. It falls under miscellaneous mechanisms for shoemaking within the footwear equipment hierarchy.
[A43D 111/006] with special means to place the shoe or shoe part in the following machine -- A43D 111/006 classifies footwear manufacturing devices that include specialized mechanisms for positioning a shoe or shoe component before it enters the next machine in the production line. The subclass covers feeding, holding, aligning or transferring means that automatically place the item for subsequent operations such as stitching, lasting or finishing. It is used to protect inventions that improve the speed, accuracy or safety of shoe-handling steps in automated shoemaking systems.
[A43D 11/12] Machines for forming the toe part or heel part of shoes, with or without use of heat -- The subclass A43D 11/12 includes machines that shape the toe or heel portion of shoes, either by mechanical forming or by applying heat. These devices work on lasts to form upper parts, counters or insoles before the pulling-over or lasting stage, facilitating preparation of the footwear's front or rear sections.
[A43D 11/14] Devices for treating shoe parts, e.g. stiffeners, with steam or liquid -- The subclass A43D 11/14 covers equipment that applies steam, water, or other liquids to shoe components such as stiffeners, uppers, counters or insoles during preliminary processing. By moistening or heating these parts, the devices make the material more pliable for shaping, assembling or lasting operations in footwear production.
[A43D 11/145] with means, e.g. transport chains, for continuously transferring the shoe parts through the machines -- This subclass pertains to footwear-manufacturing machines that use transport mechanisms such as chains to move shoe parts continuously through equipment that preliminarily treats or assembles uppers, counters or insoles on lasts before the pulling-over or lasting operations. It specifies the means for ongoing transfer of components within the assembling stage.
[A43D 113/00] Machines for making shoes with out-turned flanges of the uppers or for making moccasins -- A43D 113/00 refers to machines designed to manufacture shoes whose uppers have out-turned flanges, as well as equipment for producing moccasins. These devices belong to the footwear-manufacturing subclass within the CPC hierarchy for tools and methods used in making or repairing shoes.
[A43D 115/00] Machines for skiving or removing heel-lifts, heels, or soles, or for removing stitches, preparatory to repair -- A43D 115/00 covers machines and tools designed to skive or remove heel-lifts, heels, soles, or stitching from footwear as a preparatory step before repair, falling under equipment for manufacturing or repairing shoes.
[A43D 117/00] Racks for receiving or transporting shoes or shoe parts -- A43D 117/00 covers racks intended to receive or transport finished shoes or individual shoe parts during manufacturing, storage or distribution. The devices may be stationary or mobile and are designed to hold multiple items securely to facilitate handling, sorting or movement within shoe-making or retail environments.
[A43D 119/00] Driving or controlling mechanisms of shoe machines -- A43D 119/00 covers the drive and control systems used in machines that manufacture or repair shoes, such as motors, gear trains, hydraulic or pneumatic actuators, and electronic controllers that regulate speed, position, and timing of stitching, cutting, or forming operations. It includes mechanisms that start, stop, reverse, or synchronize various shoe-making processes.
[A43D 13/00] Machines for pulling-over the uppers when loosely laid upon the last and tacking the toe end -- This subclass covers machinery used in shoe manufacturing that automatically lifts or pulls the upper material over the last when it is loosely positioned and then secures the toe portion by tacking. It includes devices that combine pull-over and toe-tacking functions to streamline footwear assembly.
[A43D 13/02] Devices for locating caps or stiffeners on pulling-over machines -- A43D 13/02 pertains to devices that position caps or stiffeners on pulling-over machines used in shoe production. These machines lay the upper loosely on
[A43D 15/00] Pulling-over or lasting machines for binding the toe end with cord, string, or wire -- A43D 15/00 covers machines that pull over or last footwear while binding the toe end with cord, string, or wire. These devices are used in the manufacturing or repair of shoes to secure the toe area during the lasting process.
[A43D 17/00] Pulling-over or lasting machines with oscillating shoe supports -- A43D 17/00 covers machines used in footwear manufacturing that pull the upper over the sole (lasting) and incorporate oscillating shoe supports to improve placement and fit. These devices combine the pulling-over action with a moving support that adjusts the shoe during the lasting process, enhancing efficiency and precision.
[A43D 19/00] Hand lasting -- Hand lasting is the manual process of stretching and shaping a shoe upper over a foot-shaped form (the last) and securing it to the insole in preparation for sole attachment. It is a method used in footwear manufacturing and is classified under CPC A43D 19/00.
[A43D 19/02] Tacking or nailing devices for use with lasting pincers -- A43D 19/02 classifies devices that tack or nail components of shoes while using lasting pincers, a hand-lasting tool in footwear production. The entry covers specialized equipment that secures uppers to soles during the manual lasting process.
[A43D 21/00] Lasting machines -- A43D 21/00 pertains to lasting machines, which hold a shoe upper over a shaped form and apply pressure to attach the sole, shaping the footwear into its final contour.
[A43D 21/003] with lasting strings, stretching straps or the like, for forming the shank portions of shoes -- A43D 21/003 pertains to lasting machines that use lasting strings, stretching straps or comparable devices to shape the shank portion of shoes during manufacturing; it is a subcategory of equipment for producing and repairing footwear.
[A43D 21/006] with rotating lasting means -- A43D 21/006 covers lasting machines used in footwear production that employ rotating lasting means to shape the upper material around the shoe last. The rotating action improves uniform pressure and efficiency during the lasting process, distinguishing these machines from those with stationary or reciprocating lasting mechanisms.
[A43D 21/02] with one single lasting gripper -- A43D 21/02 refers to footwear lasting machines that employ a single lasting gripper, a device that holds the shoe upper onto the form during shaping. This subclass falls under the broader group for machines, tools, equipment or methods used in manufacturing or repairing footwear.
[A43D 21/04] for lasting the opposite sides of the shoe one after another -- A43D 21/04 covers machines or methods for shoe lasting that shape the opposite sides of a shoe sequentially, applying pressure to one side and then the other to form the final contour. It falls within the footwear manufacturing subgroup of the Cooperative Patent Classification, under Human Necessities → Footwear → Machines, tools, equipment or methods for manufacturing or repairing footwear.
[A43D 21/06] for wooden soles -- A43D 21/06 designates equipment for the lasting process that handles wooden soles, detailing machines or methods that shape, attach or otherwise treat a wooden sole during shoe manufacture. It is part of the subclass for machines, tools or equipment used in producing or repairing footwear.
[A43D 21/08] with last supports and toe- or heel-embracing wipers movable both horizontally and vertically -- A43D 21/08 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to lasting machines used in footwear manufacturing, specifically those that feature last supports along with toe- or heel-embracing wipers capable of moving both horizontally and vertically. This classification is part of a detailed system for organizing patents related to human necessities, including footwear machinery.
[A43D 21/10] Bed-lasting machines -- A43D 21/10 covers machines that shape or adjust shoe lasts, the forms used to construct footwear, during the manufacturing process. These bed-lasting machines are specialized equipment for creating, modifying or repairing lasts to ensure proper fit and design of shoes. They fall under the CPC hierarchy of human necessities, footwear, and manufacturing or repairing equipment.
