[A45F 5/00] Holders or carriers for hand articles -- **Glossary:** - holder or carrier: a human body-supported device for holding or carrying hand articles to facilitate transport and/or hands-free carrying of the hand article, where the hand article is not in use or being passively used while supported by the wearer. For example, an armband, a shoulder-strap or a belt clip, each including means for retaining a hand article. The holder or carrier is not part of the hand article itself, e.g. it is distinct from the housing of an electronic device. Holders or carriers do not include sacks or packs.

[A45F 5/1541] Holders or carriers for eyeglasses or spectacles -- A45F 5/1541 classifies accessories that hold or carry eyeglasses or spectacles, such as cases, pouches, or straps, intended for personal transport and protection while traveling. This falls under the broader group of hand-carried travelling equipment.
[A45F 5/155] Holders or carriers for thin, flat, rectangular articles, e.g. ID badges or cards -- A45F 5/155 pertains to holders or carriers specifically shaped to contain thin, flat, rectangular items such as identification badges or cards. These devices are intended for body-mounted or travel-related equipment, providing a convenient way to secure and display the articles.
[A45F 5/1558] Holders or carriers for keys -- A45F 5/1558 covers devices that hold or carry keys, such as keychains, key rings or pocket key holders intended for personal use while travelling or worn on the body. These items fall under travelling or camp equipment for hand articles.
[A45F 5/1566] Holders or carriers for tubular, rod-shaped articles, e.g. batons -- This subclass relates to holders or carriers that secure tubular or rod-shaped articles such as batons, typically integrated into bags, straps or other body-carried equipment. It falls within the travelling or camp equipment group for sacks or packs carried on the body.
[A45F 5/1575] Holders or carriers for portable tools -- A45F 5/1575 covers devices that hold or carry portable tools on the body, such as tool belts, pocketed pouches, or integrated compartments in backpacks and other body-worn packs. The classification applies to any arrangement designed to secure hand tools for travel or work while keeping them readily accessible. It falls under travelling or camp equipment for sacks or packs carried on the body.
[A45F 5/1583] Holders or carriers for beverage vessels, e.g. bottles -- This CPC subclass covers devices that hold or carry beverage vessels such as bottles and are designed to be worn on the body. It is part of the travelling or camp equipment group, specifically sacks or packs carried on the body, within the broader category of hand-or-travelling articles.

[A46] BRUSHWARE -- A46 includes items used for personal grooming and hygiene such as brushes, combs, hair-care implements and related accessories. The class covers devices for cleaning, styling or maintaining hair, skin, nails and oral cavities, together with their components and manufacturing methods.
[A46B] BRUSHES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus with brushes in general  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Body washing implements -> A47K7/00 - Handle constructions -> B25G1/00 - Attaching handles to the implements -> B25G3/00 - Vehicle cleaning apparatus not integral with vehicles -> B60S3/06 - Street cleaning -> E01H1/00  **Glossary:** - Tuft: a group of bristles
[A46B 1/00] Brush bodies and bristles moulded as a unit -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The brush body or brush carrier and the bristles are moulded in one piece  EP0474934
[A46B 11/00] Brushes with reservoir or other means for applying substances, e.g. paints, pastes, water -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Driven brush bodies -> A46B13/00 - Massage apparatus with liquid delivery -> A61H7/00
[A46B 11/0003] containing only one dose of substance, e.g. single-use toothbrushes -- A46B 11/0003 covers brushes that incorporate a single dose of a substance, such as a disposable toothbrush that includes a pre-loaded amount of toothpaste or other material for one-time use. The code falls under the brushware section for brushes with a reservoir or similar applicator, defining items designed for a single application before disposal.
[A46B 11/0006] specially adapted to feed the bristle upper surface -- This CPC subgroup covers brushes that incorporate a mechanism to supply material directly to the bristle tips, such as reservoirs or channels that feed paint, paste or water onto the bristle upper surface, allowing continuous application
[A46B 11/001] with integral reservoirs  A46B11/0003 ,  A46B11/0006  take precedence -- A46B 11/001 covers brushes that incorporate an integral reservoir or other means for holding and delivering substances such as paint, paste, or water directly to the bristles. The design enables continuous application without separate containers and falls under Human Necessities → Brushware → Brushes, with the more specific groups A46B 11/0003 and A46B 11/0006 taking precedence.
[A46B 11/0013] dispensing by gravity or by shaking -- A46B 11/0013 classifies brushes that incorporate a built-in reservoir and release the loaded substance onto the bristles by gravity or by shaking the brush, enabling paint, paste or water to flow without external applicators.
[A46B 11/0017] with pre-pressurised reservoirs, e.g. aerosols -- A46B 11/0017 classifies brushes that incorporate a pre-pressurised reservoir, such as an aerosol can, for dispensing paints, pastes, water or similar substances during use. The subclass falls under Human Necessities → Brushware → Brushes and specifically addresses brush designs where the supply of the coating material is stored under pressure for direct application.
[A46B 11/002] pressurised at moment of use manually or by powered means -- A46B 11/002 covers brushes that include a built-in reservoir and a mechanism that pressurises the material at the moment of use, either by manual actuation or by a powered device, to deliver paints, pastes, water or similar substances directly onto a surface.
[A46B 11/0024] with a permanently displaceable pressurising member that remain in position unless actuated, e.g. lead-screw or ratchet mechanisms, toothpaste tube twisting or rolling devices -- This subclass covers handheld brushes that integrate a material reservoir and a permanently positioned pressurising element-such as a lead-screw, ratchet, or similar mechanism-that stays locked until the user actuates it, for example by twisting or rolling, to dispense paints, pastes, toothpaste or other substances.
[A46B 11/0027] Lead-screw mechanisms -- A46B 11/0027 covers lead-screw mechanisms employed in brush applicators that incorporate a reservoir or similar supply system for paints, pastes, water or other substances. The classification relates to devices where a threaded spindle drives the brush or adjusts pressure, feed rate, or positioning of the applicator head, enabling precise control of material delivery during use.
[A46B 11/0031] Means for influencing rotation of the knob, e.g. ratchet, click mechanisms, one way clutches -- This subclass covers devices that modify how the knob of a reservoir-type brush rotates, such as ratchet or click mechanisms and one-way clutches, allowing controlled or limited turning of the brush during application of paints, pastes, or water.
[A46B 11/0034] Means comprising a rachet mechanism or equivalent for advancing the piston stepwise -- The A46B 11/0034 entry covers brush tools that contain a built-in reservoir for paints, pastes or water and use a ratchet-type mechanism to move the piston in incremental steps, allowing controlled, repeatable application of the material.
[A46B 11/0037] A piston or plunger advanced by direct pushing along the side of brush handle -- This subclass covers brushes that incorporate a piston or plunger moved by direct pressure along the side of the handle to dispense material from a built-in reservoir, allowing controlled application of paints, pastes, or water.
[A46B 11/0041] Flexible or deformable reservoirs, e.g. resilient bulbs, compressible tubes -- This subclass covers brushes that incorporate a flexible or deformable reservoir for holding liquids, pastes or paints, such as resilient bulbs or compressible tubes that release the substance onto the bristles. The reservoir is typically integral to the brush and designed to supply the coating medium during use, falling under the brushware group of human necessities.
[A46B 11/0044] where a tube is rolled up transverse to long axis -- A46B 11/0044 covers brushes that include a tube rolled up across the brush width, perpendicular to the bristles, to act as a reservoir for liquids such as paint or water. The rolled tube runs transverse to the brush's long axis and supplies the substance to the applicator surface during use. This subclass falls under brushes with a built-in reservoir within the broader brushware hierarchy.
[A46B 11/0048] where a tube is twisted along length -- This subclass covers brushes that incorporate a twisted tube running along the length of the brush to form a reservoir for delivering liquids such as paint, paste or water onto the bristles.
[A46B 11/0051] Peristaltic pump
[A46B 11/0055] with a reciprocating piston or plunger acting as the pressurising means -- A46B 11/0055 covers brush-type applicators that include an internal reservoir and use a reciprocating piston or plunger to pressurise and deliver substances such as paint, paste or water onto the brush during operation. The piston creates a controlled flow from the container through the brush, enabling consistent application without separate pumping devices.
[A46B 11/0058] with a metered dosage -- A46B 11/0058 covers brushes that incorporate a reservoir and a metered dosage system, allowing controlled release of liquids such as paints, pastes or water while brushing. This type of brush combines the function of a standard brush with precise dispensing of the applied substance.
[A46B 11/0062] Brushes where the reservoir is specifically intended for being refilled when empty -- A46B 11/0062 covers brushes that incorporate a built-in reservoir designed to be refilled with paint, paste, water or other liquids after it is emptied. These brushes are intended for repeated use, allowing the user to
[A46B 11/0065] Brushes where the reservoir is specifically intended for being replaced when empty -- A46B 11/0065 covers hand-held brushes that incorporate a reservoir for paint, paste, water or similar liquids, where the reservoir is designed to be removed and refilled or replaced once empty. The classification applies to brushes whose refillable container is an integral, replaceable part of the tool.
[A46B 11/0068] Brushes where the reservoir is specifically intended for solid substance that dissolves gradually -- This subclass covers brushes that incorporate a built-in reservoir intended to hold a solid substance which dissolves gradually during use, providing a continuous supply of paint, paste or other material without the need for frequent reloading.
[A46B 11/0072] Details -- A46B 11/0072 pertains to specific details of brushes designed with reservoirs or other mechanisms for applying substances such as paints, pastes, or water. This classification covers aspects like the structure and function of these application tools within the broader category of brushware for human necessities.
[A46B 11/0075] Means integral with the brush for opening, puncturing or piercing sealed reservoirs, e.g. on insertion -- A46B 11/0075 classifies brushes that incorporate an integral device for opening, puncturing or piercing a sealed reservoir, enabling the release of paint, paste, water or similar substances when the brush is inserted. This subclass falls under brushes with reservoirs used for applying substances.
[A46B 11/0079] Arrangements for preventing undesired leakage or dispensing -- This subclass concerns brushware that includes a reservoir for paint, paste, water or similar substances and features a mechanism-such as a seal, valve, valve-like geometry or other structural element-to prevent unintended leakage or uncontrolled dispensing of the material during use. It covers any arrangement that controls the flow from the brush while maintaining ready access to the supplied medium.
[A46B 11/0082] Means for closing, sealing or controlling the flow using capillary action -- A46B 11/0082 refers to brushes equipped with a liquid reservoir that employ capillary action to close, seal or regulate the flow of substances such as paints, pastes or water. The subclass covers designs where wicking channels, porous elements or capillary ducts are integrated into the brush head or handle to control fluid delivery during use.
[A46B 11/0086] Means for closing or sealing, e.g. toothpaste outlet when brush not in use thereby preventing leakage or drying out -- A46B 11/0086 pertains to brush designs that incorporate a means for closing or sealing the applicator opening, such as a mechanism that shuts off a toothpaste outlet when the brush is not in use, thereby preventing leakage or drying out.
[A46B 11/0089] Caps or covers for bristles -- Caps or covers for bristles are protective elements that fit over the bristled end of a brush, shielding the fibers from drying, contamination, or mechanical damage during storage or transport. They are typically made of plastic, metal or fabric and may be snap-on, screw-on or elastic-fit, ensuring the brush retains its performance for future use.
[A46B 11/0093] Arrangements for catching drips or overflow -- A46B 11/0093 pertains to brushes that incorporate mechanisms for collecting excess liquid, such as drip trays or overflow channels, to prevent drips or overflow during application of paints, pastes, or water.
[A46B 11/0096] Indication of the level of the remaining fluid, e.g. through transparent walls -- A feature of brushes with built-in reservoirs that shows how much liquid remains, typically by using a transparent wall or window to display the fluid level.
[A46B 11/06] connected to supply pipe -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Brush with a single dose substance -> A46B11/0003 - Specially adapted to feed bristles upper surface -> A46B11/0006
[A46B 11/063] by means of a supply pipe -- A46B 11/063 covers brushes that incorporate a supply pipe to deliver paints, pastes or other substances directly to the brush during use. It is a subcategory of brushes with a reservoir within the CPC hierarchy under Human Necessities, Brushware, Brushes.
[A46B 11/066] with means for introducing an additive, e.g. soap -- This subclass covers brushes that incorporate a system for dispensing an additive such as soap while the brush is used, typically through a built-in reservoir or channel that mixes the additive with water or paint.
[A46B 11/08] with heating means -- A46B 11/08 covers brushes that incorporate a heating element to warm the material being applied, such as paint, paste or water, improving flow or drying. The heating means may be built into the brush body, handle or reservoir, and is intended for use with brushes that have a reservoir or other means for applying substances.
[A46B 13/00] Brushes with driven brush bodies -- A46B 13/00 covers brushes whose brush bodies are driven by an external power source, such as electric or pneumatic motors, rather than being manually operated. The classification includes designs where the brush head or bristles are rotated or vibrated by a built-in drive mechanism to enhance cleaning or polishing performance. It falls under the broader categories of human necessities, brushware and brushes.
[A46B 13/001] Cylindrical or annular brush bodies -- A46B 13/001 covers brush bodies that are cylindrical or annular in shape, designed to hold and support bristles or other cleaning elements while being driven by a motor or other power source. These components are used in devices such as electric toothbrushes, floor scrubbers, and other powered cleaning tools. The code falls under the brushware group within the human necessities section.
[A46B 13/003] made up of a series of annular brush rings; Annular brush rings therefor -- A46B 13/003 relates to brushes whose bodies are constructed from a series of annular, or ring-shaped, brush segments that are driven to rotate as a unit. The classification covers brush assemblies in which the brush rings are arranged concentrically or in series to form the working surface, commonly found in cleaning or polishing devices.
[A46B 13/005] made up of a series of longitudinal strips or segments -- A46B 13/005 classifies brushes whose brush bodies consist of a series of longitudinal strips or segments, rather than a solid mass, defining the construction of the brush element used in cleaning or personal-care tools.
[A46B 13/006] formed by winding a strip tuft in a helix about the body -- The subclass A46B 13/006 covers brushes whose bristles are formed by helically winding a strip tuft around the brush body, creating a continuous, spiral-shaped brush surface. This manufacturing method is applied to various brush types used in cleaning, personal care and similar human-necessity applications.
[A46B 13/008] Disc-shaped brush bodies -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The bristle carrier or body forms a disk and the bristles are positioned on a face of said disk.
[A46B 13/02] power-driven -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Manually actuated mechanism for generating the movement of the brush -> A46B11/08
[A46B 13/023] with means for inducing vibration to the bristles -- A46B 13/023 covers brushes that include a mechanism to vibrate the bristles, such as an electric motor, piezoelectric element or other drive. The induced oscillation is intended to improve cleaning, stimulate the skin or aid product application, and applies to any brush with a driven brush body.
[A46B 13/026] Brushes which automatically reverse direction of rotation, e.g. using gravity switches -- Brushes that incorporate a mechanism to automatically reverse their direction of rotation, typically using a gravity-activated switch, allowing the brush body to change travel direction without manual adjustment.
[A46B 13/04] with reservoir or other means for supplying substances -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Brush with reservoir -> A46B11/00
[A46B 13/06] with brush driven by the supplied medium -- A46B 13/06 classifies brushes whose bristle or cleaning element is set in motion by the same medium that powers the device, such as water, air, steam or another fluid supplied to the system, rather than by a separate mechanical drive. This includes devices where the driving fluid directly imparts motion to the brush body, enabling cleaning or polishing actions without additional moving parts.
[A46B 13/08] hand-driven -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Simple flexion or deformation of the brush head -> A46B5/0025
[A46B 15/00] Other brushes -- A46B 15/00 relates to brushes that do not fall into more specific categories within the brush subclass. It covers a wide range of miscellaneous brush types, such as hair- or cosmetic brushes, cleaning brushes for surfaces or equipment, and other specialized or generic brush designs used in household, industrial or personal-care applications. This entry groups together brush configurations that are not otherwise detailed in the CPC hierarchy.
[A46B 15/0002] Arrangements for enhancing monitoring or controlling the brushing process -- A46B 15/0002 covers devices, systems or methods that improve the observation, measurement or regulation of a brush's operation, such as sensors, feedback loops or control electronics that monitor speed, pressure, coverage or other parameters. The aim is to enhance accuracy, consistency or safety of the brushing process in applications ranging from personal hygiene to industrial cleaning.
[A46B 15/0004] with a controlling means -- A46B 15/0004 covers brushes that incorporate a controlling means, such as an adjustable, motorised or otherwise regulated mechanism that governs the brush's movement, pressure or operation. The subclass applies to "other brushes" where the user can modify or control the brush's action during use.
[A46B 15/0006] with a controlling brush technique device, e.g. stroke movement measuring device -- A46B 15/0006 covers brushes that incorporate a device for controlling the brushing technique, for example a mechanism that measures or monitors the stroke movement during use.
[A46B 15/0008] with means for controlling duration, e.g. time of brushing -- A46B 15/0008 covers brushes that incorporate a feature for limiting or timing the brushing period, such as a built-in timer or other duration-control mechanism. The code is used for devices where the user can set how long the brush operates.
[A46B 15/001] with means indicating the remaining useful life of brush -- A46B 15/001 covers brushes that incorporate a means to show how much useful life remains, such as wear-visible tips, color-changing fibers or mechanical counters that signal when the brush should be replaced. The feature helps users maintain performance and avoid damage in cleaning, painting or other brush-based tasks.
[A46B 15/0012] with a pressure controlling device -- A46B 15/0012 refers to brushes - other than specialized industrial types - that incorporate a pressure-controlling device to adjust the force applied during use, such as mechanisms that regulate brushing pressure for personal or household brushware.
[A46B 15/0014] with a controlling temperature device -- A46B 15/0014 covers brushes that incorporate a device to control temperature, such as heating or cooling elements, used in personal or household applications. The classification applies to any brush-excluding specialized industrial types-where the temperature feature is integral to its function, for example hair-brushes with warming technology or cleaning brushes that maintain heat.
[A46B 15/0016] with enhancing means -- A46B 15/0016 classifies brushes that incorporate additional features or mechanisms to improve their performance, such as adjustable bristle density, cleaning aids or other functional augmentations. It is used for patents describing "brushes with enhancing means" within the broader categories of brushware and other brushes.
[A46B 15/0018] with antistatic properties -- A46B 15/0018 classifies brushes that are designed to have antistatic properties, covering any brush type whose construction or material treatment reduces static electricity buildup during use. This subclass falls under the hierarchy human necessities → brushware → brushes → other brushes. It is used by the EPC and USPTO to index patents that address static control in brush designs.
[A46B 15/002] with a means for removing or creating static electricity -- The class covers brushes that incorporate devices to dissipate or generate static electricity, such as antistatic bristles, conductive elements, or built-in charge generators. These features are used in personal hygiene, cleaning or industrial tools to control static charge buildup.
[A46B 15/0022] with an electrical means -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Powered brushes -> A46B13/02 - Electric toothbrushes -> A61C17/16
[A46B 15/0024] with means generating ions -- A46B 15/0024 covers brush devices that incorporate means for generating ions, such as hair or cosmetic brushes equipped with ion-emitting elements to reduce static charge or enhance product performance. The classification applies to any brush in the "other brushes" subgroup that uses ionisation technology.
[A46B 15/0026] with a magnetic means -- A46B 15/0026 covers brushes that incorporate a magnetic element, such as a magnet or an electrically-powered magnetic field, to capture, hold, or manipulate ferromagnetic particles during cleaning, polishing, or surface-finishing operations. The magnetic means may be integral to the brush head or added as an accessory, enabling the brush to attract debris or abrasive media for more effective treatment of the workpiece.
[A46B 15/0028] with an acoustic means -- The CPC A46B 15/0028 classification covers brushware that incorporates an acoustic device, such as a squeaker, bell or other sound-producing element, enabling the brush to emit noise during use, often for hygiene or sensory feedback.
[A46B 15/003] Enhancing with heat -- A46B 15/003 covers brushes that incorporate a heating function, such as electric or thermal elements designed to warm the bristles during use. The heat may improve cleaning efficiency, soften debris, or provide therapeutic benefits, and the classification applies to any brush type not otherwise specified.
[A46B 15/0032] with protrusion for polishing teeth -- A46B 15/0032 covers brushes that have a protruding element designed specifically for polishing teeth. These are classified under brushware and other brushes, indicating their use in oral hygiene rather than general cleaning. The protrusion may be a hard tip or abrasive surface to aid in removing plaque and stains.
[A46B 15/0034] with a source of radiation, e.g. UV, IR, LASER, X-ray for irradiating the teeth and associated surfaces -- A46B 15/0034 covers brushes used in oral care that incorporate a source of radiation-such as ultraviolet, infrared, laser or X-ray-to irradiate teeth and related surfaces for cleaning, disinfection or therapeutic purposes.
[A46B 15/0036] with a lighting means, e.g. laser, bulb  A46B15/0034  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Brush with a radiation source -> A46B15/0034
[A46B 15/0038] with signalling means -- A46B 15/0038 designates brushes that incorporate signalling means, such as visual or audible indicators that warn of wear, improper use, or the need for cleaning. It falls under Human necessities → Brushware → Brushes → Other brushes and covers any brush where an integrated signal enhances safety or maintenance.
[A46B 15/004] with an acoustic signalling means, e.g. noise -- A46B 15/004 classifies brushes that incorporate an acoustic signalling device, such as a noise-producing element, to alert users or draw attention. It falls under the hierarchy HUMAN NECESSITIES → BRUSHWARE → BRUSHES → Other brushes within the Cooperative Patent Classification used by the EPO and USPTO.
[A46B 15/0042] with musical signalling means -- A46B 15/0042 covers brushes that incorporate musical signalling means, such as brushes equipped with built-in bells, whistles or electronic sound devices to alert users. These are classified under brushware within the human necessities category.

[A46B 15/0046] with vibrating signalling means -- A46B 15/0046 covers brushes that integrate a vibrating signalling device, such as a motor or piezo element, to provide tactile or audible alerts during use. The feature is applied to miscellaneous brush types classified under "Other brushes."
[A46B 15/0048] Drip collector, e.g. collecting the liquids dripping from the brush during a brushing process -- A46B 15/0048 covers devices that collect liquid that drips from a brush during use, such as a catcher or tray positioned beneath the bristles to capture runoff. The element is used with various brushes and is classified under human necessities → brushware → brushes → other brushes.

[A46B 15/0055] Brushes combined with other articles normally separate from the brushing process, e.g. combs, razors, mirrors -- A46B 15/0055 covers brushes that are integrally combined with other items normally separate from the brushing action, such as combs, razors or mirrors, creating multifunctional personal-care tools.
[A46B 15/0057] Brushes fitted with a device for cleaning the ears -- A46B 15/0057 covers brushes that incorporate a device for cleaning the ears, such as ear-cleaning brushes with built-in tips or attachments designed to remove earwax. [A46B 15/0059] Brushes with a comb -- A46B 15/0059 covers brushes that incorporate a comb element, such as hair-brushes or pet-brushes where the bristles are combined with spaced teeth to detangle or separate fibers. [A46B 15/0061] with a container for accessories, e.g. pills, polish, condoms, cells  A46B11/00  takes precedence -- This classification code pertains to brushes that come with a container designed to hold various accessories such as pills, polish, condoms, or batteries, though it is noted that if the brush also falls under the category of having a handle or holder (A46B11/00), that classification takes precedence.
[A46B 15/0063] Brushes with a device for opening containers, e.g. shoes polish  A46B11/0093  takes precedence -- A46B 15/0063 covers brushes that integrate a device for opening the product container, for example a built-in opener for shoe-polish tins. The subclass is used when the brush itself provides the opening function, but it is overridden by the broader A46B11/0093, which takes precedence.
[A46B 15/0065] Brush with an additional cutting tool, e.g. nail clippers, hair cutting -- A46B 15/0065 covers brushes that incorporate a built-in cutting element, such as integrated nail-clippers or hair-cutting blades. The classification groups these hybrid tools under the "Other brushes" subcategory of the brushware segment in the Human Necessities class. This enables patents for combined grooming and brushing devices to be indexed together.
[A46B 15/0067] Brushes fitted with a drinking device, i.e. brushes with devices allowing drinking -- A46B 15/0067 designates brushes that incorporate a drinking device, meaning the brush includes an integrated mechanism allowing the user to drink while using the brush. [A46B 15/0073] Brushes with an arrangement for using the floss -- A46B 15/0073 relates to brushes that integrate a mechanism or feature for holding and using dental floss, allowing simultaneous brushing and flossing. [A46B 15/0075] Brushes with an additional massage device -- This subclass covers brushes that incorporate an additional massage mechanism-such as vibrating, pulsating or pressure-delivery elements-intended for personal grooming or therapeutic skin stimulation. It falls within the brushware group of human necessities.
[A46B 15/0077] Brushes fitted with a mirror -- A46B 15/0077 covers brushes that incorporate a reflective mirror, typically for personal grooming or cosmetic use, enabling the user to view the area being brushed. The design integrates the mirror into the brush head or handle for convenient inspection.
[A46B 15/0079] Brushes fitted with a razor or other shaving equipments -- A46B 15/0079 covers brushes that incorporate a razor or other shaving devices, such as combined shaving brush-razor tools used for personal grooming. The classification groups these hybrid implements with other brushes that are not standard cleaning or painting types.
[A46B 15/0081] Brushes with a scraper, e.g. tongue scraper -- A46B 15/0081 refers to brushes that incorporate a scraper, such as a tongue scraper, designed for cleaning oral surfaces. It is classified under brushware for human necessities, specifically within the "other brushes" subgroup.
[A46B 15/0083] Brushes with a shoehorn -- A46B 15/0083 covers brush designs that incorporate a shoehorn feature, enabling the user to push shoes onto the foot while brushing. It falls within the brushware hierarchy under human necessities, specifically other brushes that combine cleaning and shoe-putting functions.
[A46B 15/0085] Brushes provided with an identification, marking device or design -- This subclass covers brushes that carry an identifier, marking device, or decorative design, such as logos, serial numbers, colour codes or pattern markings applied to the brush body, handle or bristles. It applies to any brush where the identification element is integral to the product rather than a separate accessory.
[A46B 15/0087] Brushes with decoration on or in the handle -- In the Cooperative Patent Classification, A46B 15/0087 covers brushes whose handles are ornamented by means such as engraving, painting, embossing or inlay, distinguishing them from plain-handled brushes. The subgroup belongs to brushware within the broader human necessities category.
[A46B 15/0089] Brushes with figurines such as animals on the handle -- This subclass covers hand-held brushes whose handles are ornamented with three-dimensional figurines, such as animal shapes, intended primarily for decorative or novelty purposes. The figurines are integral to the handle and not merely printed or painted.

[A46B 7/00] Bristle carriers arranged in the brush body -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Brushes for which the bristles are not directly joined/attached to the brush body but through an intermediate bristle carrier.

[A46B 7/023] where the bristle carrier retracts or collapses, i.e. for storage -- This subclass covers brushes whose bristle carrier is designed to retract, fold or collapse, allowing the brush to become compact for storage, such as telescoping or hinged mechanisms within the brush body.

[A46B 9/00] Arrangements of the bristles in the brush body -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fixation of the bristle in the body or carrier -> A46B3/00 - Powered brushes with cylindrical, annular or disk shape -> A46B13/00 - A46B13/008 - Bristle form or shape itself -> A46D1/00
[A46B 9/005] where the brushing material is not made of bristles, e.g. sponge, rubber or paper -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fixation of rubber bristles on body -> A46B3/22 - Bristles surrounded with an other material -> A46B9/065 - Bristle material -> A46D1/0207
[A46B 9/02] Position or arrangement of bristles in relation to surface of the brush body, e.g. inclined, in rows, in groups -- A46B 9/02 classifies brushes according to how the bristles are positioned or arranged on the brush body, including orientations such as inclined, organized in rows, or grouped together. It falls under the hierarchy Human Necessities → Brushware → Brushes and addresses the spatial relationship of bristles to the brush surface.
[A46B 9/021] arranged like in cosmetics brushes, e.g. mascara, nail polish, eye shadow -- The subclass A46B 9/021 covers brushes whose bristle arrangement imitates that of cosmetic applicators such as mascara, nail-polish or eye-shadow brushes.
[A46B 9/023] arranged like in hair brushes, e.g. hair treatment, dyeing, streaking -- This subclass defines brush constructions where the bristles are arranged in the same pattern as hair-brush bristles, suited for uses such as hair treatment, dyeing or streaking.
[A46B 9/025] the bristles or the tufts being arranged in an angled position relative to each other -- A46B 9/025 is a CPC subclass that covers brushes whose bristles or tufts are arranged at an angled position relative to each other within the brush body. This angled configuration is intended to enhance cleaning efficiency, distribute force, or suit particular surface shapes.
[A46B 9/026] where the surface of the brush body or carrier is not in one plane, e.g. not flat  A46B9/005  takes precedence -- A46B 9/026 covers brushes whose body or carrier surface is shaped in multiple planes rather than being flat, such as contoured or stepped designs that affect bristle arrangement. This subclass follows the broader A46B 9/005, which has priority over it.
[A46B 9/028] Bristle profile, the end of the bristle defining a surface other than a single plane or deviating from a simple geometric form, e.g. cylinder, sphere or cone -- The classification A46B 9/028 is placed under human necessities → brushware → brushes and pertains to brush designs whose bristle ends form surfaces other than a single flat plane, such as cylindrical, spherical or conical profiles.
[A46B 9/04] for toothbrushes -- A46B 9/04 relates to the specific arrangement of bristles in toothbrush heads, detailing patterns such as parallel, angled, spaced or multi-level configurations that influence cleaning performance. This subclass falls under HUMAN NECESSITIES → BRUSHWARE → BRUSHES and addresses design features unique to toothbrushes.
[A46B 9/045] specially adapted for cleaning a plurality of tooth surfaces simultaneously -- A46B 9/045 relates to toothbrushes whose bristle arrangement is designed to clean multiple tooth surfaces at the same time, such as multi-pronged or multi-headed designs that allow simultaneous brushing of several teeth. It falls within the human necessities hierarchy under brushware and brushes, focusing specifically on the configuration of the bristles.
[A46B 9/06] Arrangement of mixed bristles or tufts of bristles, e.g. wire, fibre, rubber -- The subclass A46B 9/06 relates to brushes whose bristle assembly incorporates two or more different materials-such as wire, fibre and rubber-combined in the same head. It covers the specific ways these mixed bristles or tufts are arranged to provide particular cleaning, scrubbing or cosmetic functions.
[A46B 9/065] the bristles or the tufts being embedded in a different brushing material -- This subclass covers brushes in which the bristles or tuft bundles are set into a secondary material rather than directly attached to the brush body, such as a polymer, elastomer or textile matrix that holds the bristles. The embedding modifies the brush's flexibility, wear resistance or cleaning performance.
[A46B 9/08] Supports or guides for bristles -- A46B 9/08 relates to devices that hold or direct the bristles in a brush, such as brackets, plates or other supports that position the bristle bundle within the brush body. These elements ensure the bristles are arranged correctly for effective cleaning or grooming.
[A46B 9/10] Adjustable supports -- A46B 9/10 covers adjustable supports used in brushes, defining mechanisms that allow the bristle arrangement within the brush body to be altered or positioned as needed. The classification falls under Human Necessities → Brushware → Brushes → Arrangements of the bristles in the brush body, and pertains to devices that enable users to modify brush geometry for varied cleaning or grooming tasks.
[A46B 9/12] Non-adjustable supports -- A46B 9/12 covers brush designs in which the bristle assembly is held in place by fixed, non-adjustable supports integrated into the brush body. The classification includes any permanent mounting means that secure the bristles without allowing positional or angle adjustments during use. It applies to brushes such as household or personal-care brushes where the bristle arrangement is rigidly set.
[A46D] MANUFACTURE OF BRUSHES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Tools, machines, blanks and methods for manufacturing brushes
[A46D 1/00] Bristles -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** This place does not cover: Making artificial bristles D01D , D01F  **Glossary:** In this place, the following terms or expressions are used with the meaning indicated: The term "Selection of materials for the bristles" refers to the material in which the bristle yarn is made.
[A46D 1/003] Testing of brushes -- A46D 1/003 covers procedures for evaluating brushes, including methods that assess bristle stiffness, wear resistance, cleaning efficiency and safety during use. The classification groups tests that
[A46D 1/006] Antimicrobial, disinfectant bristles, handle, bristle-carrier or packaging -- The CPC subclass A46D 1/006 pertains to brushes whose bristles, handle, bristle-carrier or packaging incorporate antimicrobial or disinfectant agents. It covers items designed to inhibit microbial growth or provide sanitising functions, falling under human necessities → brushware → manufacture of brushes → bristles.
[A46D 1/02] Bristles details -- A46D 1/02 covers the specific features of brush bristles, including their material, shape, size, cross-section, surface treatment, stiffness and the way they are arranged or secured on the brush core. The classification is used for patents that address any technical detail of bristle design or modification.
[A46D 1/0207] Bristles characterised by the choice of material, e.g. metal -- A46D 1/0207 designates brushes whose bristles are defined by the material they are made of, such as metal, and distinguishes these from other material choices. It belongs to the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Brushware → Manufacture of Brushes → Bristles.
[A46D 1/0215] Bristles characterised by the material being biodegradable -- A46D 1/0215 covers brushes whose bristles are made from biodegradable materials. It falls under the hierarchy Human Necessities
[A46D 1/0223] Bristles characterised by being electrostatically charged -- A46D 1/0223 covers brushes whose bristles are given an electrostatic charge, allowing the bristles to attract or repel particles through static electricity. This feature is used to enhance cleaning, dust-removal or material-handling performance in various brush applications.
[A46D 1/023] Bristles with at least a core and at least a partial sheath -- This subclass concerns brush bristles that are constructed with a distinct core element and a surrounding sheath that covers at least part of the core, such as fibers or filaments encased partly in a protective or functional covering. It applies to any bristle design where the core and sheath are separate but combined within the same brush element.
[A46D 1/0238] Bristles with non-round cross-section -- A46D 1/0238 pertains to brush bristles that have a non-round cross-section, such as oval, rectangular, triangular or other shaped profiles, intended to alter stiffness, cleaning efficiency or visual effect. It falls under the manufacture of brushes within the human necessities category.
[A46D 1/0246] Hollow bristles -- Hollow bristles are tubular brush filaments that contain an internal cavity, making them lighter, more flexible, or capable of delivering fluids such as cleaning agents during use. They are formed by shaping synthetic or natural fibers into a tube, typically by extrusion or wrapping, and are employed in cleaning, painting and personal-care brushes.
[A46D 1/0253] Bristles having a shape which is not a straight line, e.g. curved, "S", hook, loop -- A46D 1/0253 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers bristles with shapes that deviate from a straight line, such as curved, S-shaped, hooks, or loops, used in brush manufacturing. This classification is specific to the design and structure of non-linear bristles for enhancing functionality or performance in various brushing applications.
[A46D 1/0261] Roughness structure on the bristle surface -- This subgroup concerns brushes whose bristles are provided with a deliberately roughened or textured surface, such as micro-structures, patterned coatings or treated fibers, to improve cleaning, gripping or abrasive performance. It covers the creation or application of such roughness during bristle manufacture for enhanced interaction with workpieces or fluids.
[A46D 1/0269] Monofilament bristles -- Monofilament bristles are single-filament hair-like elements used in brushes, typically made from synthetic polymers such as nylon, polyester or natural fibers, providing uniform stiffness and wear resistance for cleaning, polishing
[A46D 1/0276] Bristles having pointed ends -- The CPC subclass A46D 1/0276 covers bristles whose tips are sharpened or otherwise formed with pointed ends to improve penetration or cleaning efficiency. These bristles are typically used in brushes for personal care, household, or industrial applications where a fine, penetrating action is required. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Brushware → Manufacture of Brushes → Bristles.
[A46D 1/0284] Bristles having rounded ends -- A46D 1/0284 designates brush bristles whose tips are formed with rounded ends, a feature that reduces wear and enhances safety during use. This subclass falls under Human Necessities → Brushware → Manufacture of Brushes → Bristles in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[A46D 1/0292] Bristles having split ends -- A46D 1/0292 covers brush bristles whose individual fibers are split at the tips, forming multiple fine filaments. The split-end design increases flexibility and surface area, improving a brush's ability to clean, smooth, or apply substances.
[A46D 1/04] Preparing bristles -- A46D 1/04 covers the processes used to prepare bristles for brush production, including cleaning, sorting, cutting, shaping, and treating raw fibers or other materials before they are assembled into finished brushes. It encompasses methods that improve bristle uniformity, stiffness, or durability, such as heat-setting, chemical treatment, or mechanical conditioning. This subclass falls under the broader category of brushware manufacturing within human necessities.
[A46D 1/045] Cleaning, e.g. washing, drying -- A46D 1/045 covers the cleaning of brushes, including methods for washing and drying bristles or other brush elements. It relates to procedures that remove contaminants, moisture or residues to restore the brush's functional condition, typically before or after use.
[A46D 1/05] Splitting -- A46D 1/05 Splitting refers to the process or apparatus used to separate individual bristles from a bundle or from a brush core during brush manufacture. This includes mechanisms that cut, pull, or otherwise divide bristle groupings to prepare them for assembly into finished brushes.
[A46D 1/055] Combing -- A46D 1/055 covers combing, including brush or comb designs and methods for arranging, separating or smoothing hair or other fibrous materials with bristles. It addresses features such as tooth geometry, spacing and motion that enable the combing action.
[A46D 1/06] Machines or apparatus for cutting bristles -- A46D 1/06 covers machines and apparatus employed to cut brush bristles during the manufacturing process. It includes devices such as shear cutters, trimmers and precision cutting tools that shape and size bristle bundles for brushes and related products.
[A46D 1/08] Preparing uniform tufts of bristles -- This subcategory covers methods and devices for creating bristle tufts with consistent length, density and alignment for brushes, including techniques for cutting, arranging and bundling the bristles to achieve uniformity.
[A46D 1/10] Vibrating devices for arranging bristles -- A46D 1/10 covers vibrating mechanisms used in brush manufacturing to position or align the bristles before fixing them in place. These devices apply controlled oscillations to ensure uniform distribution of bristles on the brush head, improving performance and consistency.
[A46D 3/00] Preparing, brush bodies -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and devices for making brushes.

[A46D 3/02] Machines for drilling bodies -- A46D 3/02 covers machines used to drill holes or cavities in brush blanks or handles as part of preparing brush bodies, enabling insertion of bristles, pins or other components during brush production. The subgroup includes rotary or reciprocating drilling equipment designed for the various materials and shapes encountered in brush manufacturing.
[A46D 3/04] Machines for inserting or fixing bristles in bodies -- A46D 3/04 classifies machines that insert or secure bristles into brush bodies, including equipment that feeds, positions, anchors, or bonds bristle tufts to handles or other substrates during brush manufacture. These devices automate the assembly step that creates the functional brush head.
[A46D 3/042] for fixing bristles using an anchor or staple -- A46D 3/042 concerns methods and devices for securing bristles to brush bodies by means of an anchor or staple, detailing the attachment process used in the manufacture of brushes. It is part of the brushware sub-group within human necessities.
[A46D 3/045] for fixing bristles by fusing or gluing to a body -- A46D 3/045 relates to methods of fixing bristles to a brush body by means of fusing or gluing. The entry falls under the brushware segment of the CPC, specifically within the manufacture of brushes category.
[A46D 3/047] for clamping or gluing bristles into rings, e.g. paint brushes, brooms -- A46D 3/047 covers methods and devices for securing bristles to brush bodies, specifically clamping or gluing bristles into rings for items such as paint brushes, brooms and similar tools. It falls under the brushware subgroup of the human necessities hierarchy.
[A46D 3/05] for fixing the bristles between wires, tapes, or the like -- A46D 3/05 covers methods and devices for securing brush bristles to the brush body by interlacing them with wires, tapes, or similar binding materials. It includes techniques that tie, stitch, or wrap bristles to form a stable brush structure, distinct from adhesives or mechanical fasteners that do not use such flexible connectors. This subclass falls under the manufacture of brushes within the broader brushware category.
[A46D 3/06] Machines for both drilling bodies and inserting bristles -- A46D 3/06 refers to machines that simultaneously drill the bodies of brushes and insert the bristles, used in the manufacture of brushes. It falls under the Human Necessities section, within Brushware and the Manufacture of Brushes subclass, covering equipment that prepares brush bodies and loads them with bristles.
[A46D 3/062] of the carousel or drum type  A46D3/067  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Featuring simultaneous pivoting of bodies for drilling and filling -> A46D3/067
[A46D 3/065] of the belt or chain type  A46D3/067  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Featuring simultaneous pivoting of bodies for drilling and filling -> A46D3/067
[A46D 3/067] featuring simultaneous pivoting of bodies for drilling and filling -- A46D 3/067 relates to brush manufacturing, specifically designs where the brush bodies can pivot simultaneously to perform drilling and filling operations. The feature enables coordinated movement of multiple brush elements during the creation of holes and the subsequent injection of material, improving efficiency and precision in brush production.
[A46D 3/08] Parts of brush-making machines -- The CPC entry A46D 3/08 designates the parts of machines used to manufacture brushes, covering components of brush-making equipment employed in preparing brush bodies. It sits within Human Necessities → Brushware → Manufacture of Brushes.
[A46D 3/082] Magazines for bristles; Feeding bristles to magazines; Knot picking -- A46D 3/082 covers devices that store and supply brush bristles during brush production, such as magazines or hoppers, and mechanisms for feeding the bristles from these magazines while also addressing techniques for removing bristle knots. It is used for classifying brush-making equipment that handles bristle handling and knot-picking operations.
[A46D 3/085] Magazines for brush bodies; Feeding bodies -- The subclass relates to magazines that store pre-formed brush bodies and the mechanisms that feed these bodies to a brush-making machine. It includes devices that hold and supply brush bodies sequentially for assembly or further processing.
[A46D 3/087] Holders or supports for brush bodies -- A46D 3/087 covers devices used to hold or support brush bodies during preparation or assembly, such as clamps, jigs, fixtures and other temporary supports that secure the brush while it is being manufactured.
[A46D 5/00] Devices for preparing quills for use as carriers for bristles -- A46D 5/00 covers tools and apparatus designed to shape, cut, or otherwise prepare quill shafts so they can serve as carriers for brush bristles, enabling the assembly of hand-made brushes. These devices typically trim, hollow, or form the quill to receive and secure the bristle bundle.
[A46D 7/00] Pressing devices for making brooms composed of brushwood or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Tools or elements for pressing the bristles together generaly on the anchoring side in the process of manufacturing/assembling a broom.
[A46D 9/00] Machines for finishing brushes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines for finishing or testing completed brushes.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Bristles; Selection of materials for bristles -> A46D1/00 - A46D1/10 - Preparing, Manufacturing brush bodies -> A46D3/00
[A46D 9/02] Cutting -- A46D 9/02 refers to machines used in the finishing stage of brush production that perform cutting operations, such as trimming or shaping bristles to achieve a specified length or profile. It is classified under Human necessities → Brushware → Manufacture of brushes → Machines for finishing brushes.
[A46D 9/025] Deflecting parts of the bristle field in order to trim the rest -- A46D 9/025 covers machines used in brush manufacturing that deflect portions of the bristle field to enable trimming of the remaining bristles, improving uniformity and shape. It falls within the hierarchy Human Necessities → Brushware → Manufacture of Brushes → Machines for Finishing Brushes.
[A46D 9/04] Cleaning -- A46D 9/04 designates machines used to clean brushes in the manufacturing process, covering equipment that removes debris, oil, or other residues from bristles. It includes cleaning stations, ultrasonic or solvent baths, air-blowing devices and similar apparatus employed after brush formation. This subclass falls under Human Necessities → Brushware → Manufacture of Brushes → Machines for finishing brushes.
[A46D 9/06] Impregnating -- The A46D 9/06 subclass concerns machines used in brush finishing that impregnate the brush with liquids, resins, oils or other agents to enhance durability, stiffness or other functional properties. It includes equipment that delivers the impregnating material uniformly into the bristles or body of the brush during manufacturing. This process follows the broader categories of brush manufacture and finishing within the Human Necessities field.
[A46D 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- A46D 99/00 is a residual group in the brushware subclass that captures brush-related products or features not covered by any of the defined groups in the subclass. It is applied when the subject matter cannot be classified under the more specific categories of the brushware hierarchy.

[A47B 2001/035] the extension leaves being unfoldable -- A47B 2001/035 covers furniture, particularly tables, that incorporate extension leaves which can be unfolded to increase the tabletop surface. The claim pertains to mechanisms allowing the leaves to be deployed and secured in an extended position, enabling flexible sizing of the work or dining area.
[A47B 2001/053] and liftable to the same height as the main table leaf -- A47B 2001/053 covers tables that have a supplementary leaf which can be lifted so that its surface aligns with the height of the main table top, creating a seamless, extended work or dining surface. This feature is typically seen in adjustable or extendable tables where the leaf is hinged or mounted on a lifting mechanism.
[A47B 2001/085] with draw-leaf supported by additional legs -- A47B 2001/085 covers tables that incorporate a pull-out leaf supported by additional legs, which are deployed when the leaf is extended to provide stable reinforcement. The entry applies to designs where the extra legs may be hinged, folding or removable, enabling the leaf to be used without compromising the table's overall strength.
[A47B 2001/105] with rack and pinion gearing -- A47B 2001/105 covers tables and similar work surfaces that incorporate rack-and-pinion gearing, typically used for height-adjustable or movable furniture. The classification applies to designs where the gear mechanism provides precise, smooth positioning of the table top or portions thereof. It is a subgroup of A47B, which deals with various types of tables.
[A47B 2003/004] of the umbrella type -- A47B 2003/004 classifies items that are of the umbrella type, i.e., portable devices with a canopy supported by a collapsible frame used for protection against rain or sun. It includes folding, automatic and manual umbrellas, parasols and similar protective coverings.
[A47B 2003/006] having X/Y-crossed legs rotating around a central vertical axis -- A47B 2003/006 classifies furniture components whose legs are arranged in an X or Y pattern and are mounted to rotate about a central vertical axis, such as swivel chairs, rotating tables or similar devices where the crossed-leg frame turns while the seat or work surface remains stationary.
[A47B 2003/008] the underframe parts being stored in recesses in the underside of the table top -- A47B 2003/008 pertains to table designs in which structural or functional elements of the underframe are concealed within recesses formed underneath the tabletop, providing hidden storage or integration of components while maintaining a clean surface appearance.
[A47B 2003/025] pivoting to a position underneath the tabletop -- A47B covers furniture and its components, and the 2003/025 subclass specifies tables whose support elements pivot to a position underneath the tabletop, allowing the surface to lie flat for compact storage or transport. This mechanism typically involves folding or rotating legs that fold up and tuck beneath the top when not in use.
[A47B 2003/045] and crossed legs -- CPC A47B 2003/045 covers chairs or other seating items whose legs are arranged in a crossed or criss-cross configuration, typically for aesthetic or structural purposes. This subclass is applied to designs where the leg members intersect or are linked in a crossing pattern.
[A47B 2003/0806] and the table top rotating around a horizontal axis -- A47B 2003/0806 relates to tables whose tops can be turned about a horizontal pivot, enabling the surface to be rotated or tilted for easier access or positioning. This feature is used in work, drafting or dining tables where the user benefits from adjusting the tabletop angle or orientation.
[A47B 2003/0821] the leg holder being mounted to underside of the table top -- The A47B 2003/0821 entry relates to tables whose leg holder is affixed to the underside of the tabletop, providing a means to secure and support the legs from beneath the work surface.
[A47B 2003/0824] the table legs being individually collapsible against the underside of the table top -- A47B 2003/0824 covers tables whose legs can be folded or collapsed individually and stowed against the underside of the tabletop, allowing the piece to lie flat for compact storage or transport. This feature is common in portable and space-saving table designs.
[A47B 2003/0827] having means for holding U-shaped legs in storage position, i.e. parallel to the underside of the table top -- This CPC subclass covers tables whose U-shaped legs can be folded and secured parallel to the underside of the tabletop for storage, requiring a mechanism that holds the legs in that position. It is applied to portable or collapsible table designs that simplify transport and saving space.
[A47B 2003/0835] being superimposed, hinged and foldable -- The CPC entry A47B 2003/0835 covers furniture whose components are designed to be superimposed, joined by hinges and capable of folding for compact storage, such as collapsible tables, folding chairs and similar portable items. It classifies items where overlapping parts and hinge mechanisms enable the article to be folded or unfolded as needed.

This feature is typical of portable or multifunctional furniture such as fold-away dining or work tables.

It includes mechanisms that permit smooth, controllable movement of these inclined elements.

It covers the mechanisms or designs that allow the tabletop to switch between a low, informal height and a higher, formal dining height.

The classification gathers inventions where the gearbox is integral to the furniture's functional adjustment rather than a separate component.

It is a subclass of furniture fittings that describes how a panel is fastened automatically when the bolt is released from its retracted position, providing a self-locking or quick-release attachment.

[A47B 2230/0033] Expansible dowels or dowel-pins -- Expansible dowels or dowel-pins are mechanical fasteners used in table construction to join wooden components without glue or screws, allowing adjustable grip by expanding within a pre-drilled hole. This technology falls under the Furniture jointing subgroup of the Cooperative Patent Classification.

[A47B 2230/0048] Fastening elements comprising plugs, radially expansible by eccentric pins inserted therein -- This subgroup classifies fastening elements that consist of a plug which expands radially when eccentric pins are inserted, providing a mechanically adjustable joint for tables and other furniture components.

[A47B 27/14] Adjustable underframes -- In the CPC hierarchy under Human Necessities → Furniture → Tables → Drawing desks or tables, code A47B 27/14 pertains to adjustable underframes. These are the supporting structures beneath drawing desks or similar tables that can be raised, lowered or otherwise repositioned to modify the work surface height or angle for ergonomic or functional purposes.
[A47B 27/16] hydraulically adjustable -- A47B 27/16 is a CPC subgroup for drawing desks or tables whose height is varied by a hydraulic mechanism. The code falls under Human Necessities → Furniture → Tables and covers desks that use fluid pressure to raise or lower the work surface for ergonomic adjustment.

[A47B 3/0911] the struts being permanently connected to top and leg or underframe and leg -- A47B 3/0911 covers folding or stowable tables whose supporting struts are permanently attached to the tabletop and to the legs or underframe, so the struts cannot be detached or reconfigured independently of the table. [A47B 3/0917] the struts being permanently connected by pivots to the top or underframe only -- This subgroup relates to folding or stowable tables whose supporting struts are permanently joined to the tabletop or the lower frame by integral pivot joints, so the pivots remain fixed in place rather than being removable. It defines a specific construction style within the broader category of foldable furniture.
[A47B 3/0918] the struts being permanently connected by pivots to the legs only -- This subclass covers folding or stowable tables in which the supporting struts are permanently attached to the legs by pivot joints, enabling the tabletop to be collapsed or expanded while the struts remain fixed to the legs.

[A47B 57/265] clamped in discrete positions, e.g. on tubes with grooves or holes -- A47B 57/265 covers cabinets, racks or shelf units whose shelves or partitions are set in distinct, fixed positions by clamping them onto tubes that have grooves or holes. This sub-class falls under tables within furniture for human necessities.

[A47B 57/482] Tongues -- A "tongue" in this context is a protruding element or tab on a cabinet, rack or shelf unit that engages with a corresponding slot or notch to secure or release a shelf or partition at a selected height. By inserting or withdrawing the tongue, the user can quickly adjust the position of individual shelves or dividers without tools, allowing flexible interior configuration of the furniture.

[A47C] CHAIRS -- A47C relates to chairs, covering their design, construction, and accessories such as backs, seats, and legs. It includes various types like dining, office, and folding chairs, as well as mechanisms for adjustment or stacking. The group also addresses materials and methods used in manufacturing chairs.

The classification applies to any chair where fluid pressure is used to change position or provide support.

[A47C 1/03261] characterised by elastic means -- This CPC subclass A47C 1/03261 groups chairs that are characterised by elastic means, meaning they incorporate springs, elastomeric components or other stretchable elements to provide cushioning, flexibility or adjustable movement in the seat, backrest or frame. It covers designs where elasticity is a defining functional feature of the chair.
[A47C 1/03266] with adjustable elasticity -- This subclass covers chairs that incorporate mechanisms allowing the elasticity of the seat, backrest or cushion to be adjusted for varying firmness or support. [A47C 1/03272] with coil springs -- A47C 1/03272 classifies chairs that incorporate coil springs as part of their seating or back-rest mechanism, providing elastic support and cushioning. The code is used to index designs where the spring structure is integral to the chair's comfort or functional features. This falls under the broader hierarchy of human necessities, furniture, and chairs.
[A47C 1/03274] of torsion type -- A47C 1/03274 classifies chairs that use a torsion mechanism-typically a twisted rod or spring-to provide flexible support for the seat, back or armrests. These torsion-type designs allow the chair to flex under load and return to its original shape, offering a simple, durable means of cushioning or movement. It falls under the broader categories of furniture and chairs in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[A47C 1/03277] with bar or leaf springs -- This subgroup of the CPC chair category specifies chairs that use bar or leaf springs as the principal support system. The springs provide flexible, resilient support for the seat or backrest, improving comfort by distributing load across the structure.
[A47C 1/03279] of torsion type -- A47C 1/03279 classifies chairs that employ a torsion-type mechanism, typically using a twisted spring or bar to support or adjust the seat or backrest. The entry covers designs where the torsion element provides the primary load-bearing function, such as torsion-spring seats and similar constructions.
[A47C 1/03283] with fluid springs -- This subclass covers chairs that employ fluid springs, such as hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders, to provide adjustable height or recline functions. The fluid-filled mechanisms replace traditional coil or gas springs, offering smooth, load-compensating movement. Applications include office, drafting, and ergonomic seating.
[A47C 1/03288] with resilient blocks -- A47C 1/03288 covers chairs whose seat or backrest incorporates resilient blocks-discrete, spring-like or flexible inserts that provide cushioning, shock absorption or adjustable firmness. The classification is applied to designs that use these block elements instead of a continuous pad to enhance comfort or support.
[A47C 1/03294] slidingly movable in the base frame, e.g. by rollers -- This subclass covers chairs whose seat or entire unit can slide within the supporting base frame, typically using rollers or similar mechanisms to enable adjustment or repositioning while the frame remains stationary. It includes designs that allow smooth lateral or forward-backward movement of the chair.
[A47C 1/033] the coupling member being a flexible strip -- The code A47C 1/033 covers chairs in which the component that joins two parts, such as the seat and backrest or frame members, is realized as a flexible strip. This classification applies to designs that use a pliable band or strip to provide coupling while allowing limited movement.

The subclass covers designs where the backrest is separate or integrated but can move to provide varied seating positions.

[A47C 17/1655] forming top surface of the bed with non-rotatable seat cushion -- A47C 17/1655 covers the creation of a bed's upper surface by means of a fixed, non-rotating seat cushion, typically employed in sofa-bed designs where the cushion serves both as seating and as the sleeping surface without any swivel or turning mechanism.
[A47C 17/17] with coupled movement of back-rest and seat -- A47C 17/17 covers sofas whose back-rest and seat are mechanically linked so that they move together, such as reclining or reclining-type sofas where adjusting one part automatically adjusts the other. This enables coordinated tilting or reclining of the seating surface.

[A47C 17/1753] by sliding, e.g. by rollers -- A sofa designed to move across a floor by means of rollers or similar sliding mechanisms, allowing the entire seating unit to be repositioned with minimal effort. [A47C 17/1756] by pivoted linkages -- A47C 17/1756 covers sofas that incorporate pivoted linkages to enable movement or reconfiguration, such as reclining or transforming mechanisms, within the broader classification of chairs and furniture for human necessities.

[A47C 19/00] Bedsteads -- **Glossary:** - bedstead: bed frame having non-elastic support forming lowest part of the bed. - spring mattress with rigid frame: mattress having a rigid frame and elastic elements fixed to the rigid frame. Such mattress can be positioned on top of the bedstead, e.g. box-springs or directly on the floor, e.g. divan or slatted bed bases. - stuffed or fluid mattress: mattress without a rigid frame stuffed with fibrous material, foam, fluid or springs. Such mattress can be placed on top of bedstead, box-spring, or directly on the floor, e.g. futon.

[A47C 19/202] Stacking or nesting bedsteads -- A47C 19/202 covers bedsteads designed to be stacked or nested together for compact storage or transport. The classification applies to furniture where multiple beds can be placed one atop another or interlocked, allowing space-saving arrangements in homes, hotels or temporary accommodations.
[A47C 19/205] convertible, e.g. from single bed or sofa -- A47C 19/205 covers furniture that can be reconfigured between a single bed and a sofa, such as convertible beds or sofa-beds that fold or slide to change function. The classification applies to designs where the unit serves as a chair-type seat when not used as a bed.
[A47C 19/207] Mezzanine beds -- Mezzanine beds are sleeping surfaces built on an elevated platform that creates usable space underneath, often incorporated into compact or multi-functional furniture arrangements to maximize floor area in residential or public settings.
[A47C 19/22] Combinations of bedsteads with other furniture or with accessories, e.g. with bedside cabinets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Beds combined with other furniture, e.g. tables, bedside cabinets, etc.
[A47C 20/00] Head-, foot- or like rests for beds, sofas or the like -- A47C 20/00 covers accessories that provide head, foot or similar rests for sleeping or seating furniture such as beds, sofas or the like. It includes headboards, footboards, integrated cushions or adjustable supports designed to improve comfort, posture or aesthetic appearance of the furniture.

[A47C 20/021] Foot or leg supports -- A47C 20/021 classifies foot or leg supports designed to accompany chairs, sofas or similar seating, providing a raised resting surface for the feet. These accessories may be attached to or used with the furniture to improve comfort and posture. The entry falls under the hierarchy human necessities → furniture → chairs.
[A47C 20/022] for preventing the user from slipping down -- This CPC subclass covers devices integrated into headrests, footrests or similar supports for chairs, beds or sofas that are designed to keep the occupant from sliding down, such as anti-slip mechanisms or safety straps.
[A47C 20/023] Arm supports -- A47C 20/023 covers arm supports attached to chairs, sofas, beds or similar seating furniture, providing a place for the user's arms to rest. It includes fixed, pivoting or adjustable arms designed to enhance comfort and ergonomics.
[A47C 20/025] Supports specially adapted for pregnant women -- This subclass defines head, foot or similar rests incorporated in chairs or sofas that are specially shaped or adjustable to support the body of a pregnant woman, improving comfort and posture while seated.
[A47C 20/026] Supports for lying in a prone position  A47C20/025  takes precedence -- A47C 20/026 classifies furniture components designed to support a person lying in a prone position, such as cushions, pads or specially shaped rests that can be attached to or used with chairs, sofas or beds to facilitate face-down reclining or sleeping.

[A47C 20/042] by means of screw-and-nut mechanism -- A47C 20/042 covers chairs whose head-, foot-, or similar rests are adjusted using a screw-and-nut mechanism, allowing the rest to be raised, lowered, or tilted by turning a screw. This falls under the hierarchy Human Necessities → Furniture → Chairs.
[A47C 20/043] by means of peg-and-notch or pawl-and-ratchet mechanism -- A47C 20/043 classifies chairs that include head, foot or similar rests whose position is adjusted by a peg-and-notch or a pawl-and-ratchet mechanism, allowing the user to set the rest at various angles or heights.
[A47C 20/045] urged into an operative or non-operative position at an end of stretch -- A47C 20/045 relates to chairs and similar seating where a head-, foot- or like rest is moved to, and held in, either an operative or non-operative position at the end of its travel. The classification covers mechanisms that urge the rest into place or release it, such as locking or spring-loaded devices used in adjustable chairs, sofas or beds. It falls under Human Necessities → Furniture → Chairs → Head-, foot- or like rests.
[A47C 20/046] by means of a rack-and-pinion or like gearing mechanism -- A47C 20/046 covers chairs or similar seating units with head, foot or comparable rests that are adjusted by a rack-and-pinion or comparable gearing system, allowing the positions of these supports to be changed mechanically rather than manually. This classification is used for inventions that incorporate such gear-driven adjustment mechanisms in furniture.
[A47C 20/047] by friction locking means -- This subclass covers headrests, footrests or similar supports for beds, sofas and comparable furniture that are secured by friction-locking means. It is nested under Human Necessities → Furniture → Chairs in the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[A47C 20/048] by fluid means -- A47C 20/048 covers chairs, sofas or similar seating with head or foot rests that are moved or adjusted by fluid means such as hydraulic, pneumatic or other pressure-based systems, enabling automatic raising, lowering or tilting of the rests.
[A47C 20/06] the adjustable part immobilising foldable legs -- A47C 20/06 concerns the adjustable component that locks or releases foldable legs on head, foot or similar rests for beds, sofas or chairs. It covers mechanisms that immobilise the legs to keep the rest in a raised position or allow it to be folded flat.

[A47C 20/10] using rods -- A47C 20/10 refers to the use of rods in headrests, footrests, or similar supports for beds, sofas and other seating. It covers designs that incorporate one or more rods to provide structural reinforcement, adjustability or decorative elements in these resting components.
[A47C 20/12] using telescopic rods -- A47C 20/12 covers chairs whose head-, foot- or similar rests are adjusted by means of telescopic rods. The classification applies to designs that use extendable or collapsible rods to change the height or angle of the rest, allowing flexible positioning for user comfort. It falls under the broader categories of human necessities, furniture, chairs, and head- or foot-rests.
[A47C 20/14] using plate and catch -- A47C 20/14 covers head, foot or similar rests for beds, sofas or comparable furniture that are adjusted or secured by means of a plate and a catch mechanism, enabling the rest to be positioned and locked in place without tools.
[A47C 20/16] using toothed parts, e.g. racks -- A47C 20/16 pertains to head-, foot- or similar rests for chairs, beds or sofas that use toothed components such as rack mechanisms to adjust, lock or move the support. The classification captures devices where the adjustment or positioning of the rest is achieved through interlocking teeth, enabling precise and repeatable movement. It focuses on the mechanical principle of toothed parts rather than the overall shape of the rest.
[A47C 20/18] using friction gearing -- The A47C 20/18 subclass covers head-, foot- or similar rests for chairs, sofas or beds that incorporate friction gearing to provide adjustable positioning. It relates to mechanisms where friction-based gear interfaces lock the rest in place without a mechanical lock. This enables smooth, stable movement and precise setting of the rest's angle or height.

[A47C 23/00] Spring mattresses with rigid frame or forming part of the bedstead, e.g. box springs -- **Glossary:** - Bedstead: Bed frame having non-elastic support forming lowest part of the bed - Spring mattress with rigid frame: Mattress having a rigid frame and elastic elements fixed to the rigid frame. Such mattress can be positioned on top of the bedstead, e.g. box-springs or directly on the floor, e.g. divan or slatted bed bases - Stuffed or fluid mattress: Mattress without a rigid frame stuffed with fibrous material, foam, fluid or springs.Such mattress can be placed on top of bedstead, box-spring, or directly on the floor, e.g. futon.

[A47C 23/062] Slat supports -- Slat supports are structural components that hold and space the wooden or synthetic slats within a spring mattress or box-spring frame, ensuring even load distribution and preventing sagging. They may be integrated into the rigid frame or added as separate elements to reinforce the mattress support system.
[A47C 23/063] by elastic means, e.g. coil springs -- A47C 23/063 covers furniture, specifically chairs or bed components, that incorporate a spring mattress with a rigid frame and use elastic means such as coil springs for support, as found in box-spring type constructions.

[A47C 23/065] by fluid means -- This CPC subclass relates to chairs or sleeping furniture that incorporate a spring mattress with a rigid frame, such as a box spring, which is operated by fluid means. It covers devices that use hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid mechanisms to raise, lower, inflate, or otherwise adjust the mattress for support or comfort.
[A47C 23/066] by chains, ropes or belts -- This subclass concerns spring mattresses or box-spring frames that are supported, suspended or adjusted by chains, ropes or belts, typically as part of a chair or other furniture element. It covers mechanisms that use these flexible elements to hold or tension the mattress within the rigid frame.
[A47C 23/067] adjustable, e.g. in height or elasticity -- The A47C 23/067 classification covers spring mattresses that include a rigid frame or are integrated into the bedstead, such as box-spring units, where the mattress or its support can be adjusted in height, firmness or elasticity to suit user preference.
