[A47K] SANITARY EQUIPMENT -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sanitary equipment for bathrooms and their accessories, e.g.:
[A47K 1/00] Wash-stands -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wash-stands for personal hygiene or their appurtenances, e.g. holding devices or accessories.
[A47K 10/00] Body-drying implements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Body-drying implements like towels, from fabric or paper and their dispensers, hand or mechanically driven; Toilet paper and its holders and dispensers, hand or mechanically driven; Drying the body by hot air.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Hair drying -> A45D20/00 - Curative hot-air baths -> A61H33/06 - Sheet or web dispensers in general -> B65H - Electric heating elements -> H05B
[A47K 10/02] Towels -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Paper towels -> A47K10/16
[A47K 10/025] Holders; Boxes  dispensers  A47K10/24 -- A47K 10/025 covers holders, boxes and dispensing devices designed for body-drying implements such as towels, washcloths or similar items. It classifies any container or mechanism that stores, presents or releases these drying articles, typically as part of bathroom furniture or sanitary equipment.
[A47K 10/04] Towel racks -- Towel racks are wall-mounted or freestanding fixtures that support towels for easy access and drying, commonly installed in bathrooms, kitchens, gyms and similar spaces. They may consist of bars, loops, or heated elements to keep towels organized and warm.
[A47K 10/06] combined with means for drying towels -- A47K 10/06 designates sanitary furniture that incorporates an integrated means for drying towels, such as heated rails, racks or built-in dryers attached to bathroom cabinets, vanities or other body-drying implements. The classification covers any combination of these drying features with the primary furniture item, enabling the simultaneous storage and drying of towels.
[A47K 10/08] characterised by being mounted on wash-basins, baths, or the like -- A47K 10/08 covers body-drying implements that are designed to be mounted on wash-basins, baths or similar fixtures, such as towel rails, hand-dryers or drying racks fixed to these sanitary surfaces. The code falls under HUMAN NECESSITIES → FURNITURE → SANITARY EQUIPMENT → Body-drying implements.
[A47K 10/10] characterised by being mounted on cabinets, walls, doors, or the like -- The A47K 10/10 group comprises body-drying implements such as towel racks, hand-dryers or similar accessories that are permanently attached to cabinets, walls, doors or comparable surfaces, rather than being free-standing. This subclass falls under furniture in the sanitary equipment segment of the Human Necessities section.
[A47K 10/12] Grips, hooks, or the like for hanging-up towels -- A47K 10/12 covers grips, hooks and similar fittings designed to hold or hang towels, typically attached to bathroom furniture or fixtures. These components are classified under sanitary equipment within the broader furniture group of human necessities.
[A47K 10/14] Self-holding grips -- Self-holding grips are integral or attachable components on body-drying items such as towels, bathrobes or drying mitts that provide a secure, non-slipping hold for the user's hand while drying. They typically feature recessed, textured or contoured surfaces that stay in place without manual fastening, enhancing ease of use and safety in sanitary equipment. This element falls under CPC A47K 10/14 within the hierarchy of human necessities → furniture → sanitary equipment → body-drying implements.
[A47K 10/16] Paper towels -- A47K 10/16 covers paper towels, a disposable absorbent product used for drying the body after washing. It falls under the sanitary equipment subgroup of furniture within the broader human necessities class, distinguishing it from reusable towels or cloth wipes.
[A47K 10/18] Holders -- Holders are devices used to support or store body-drying implements such as towels, bathrobes or wipes. They include towel bars, hooks, racks and similar fixtures that keep the items accessible and organized, typically mounted in bathrooms or changing areas. This subcategory falls within the sanitary equipment group of the CPC system.
[A47K 10/185] Holders for boxes of tissue -- This subclass covers devices designed to support and retain a box of facial or hand tissue, keeping it upright and accessible for dispensing. The holders may be integrated with bathroom or kitchen furniture or be standalone accessories that secure the tissue package for convenient use.
[A47K 1/02] Portable toilet tables -- A47K 1/02 covers tables designed for use with portable toilets, including fold-out or detachable surfaces that provide a work or holding area while maintaining sanitation. The code applies to furniture that can be easily moved, installed or stored with the toilet unit, facilitating user convenience in temporary or mobile settings.
[A47K 10/20] for piled sheets -- A47K 10/20 refers to body-drying implements that employ piled sheets, such as towels, woven or non-woven fabric, or similar stackable sheet structures. The subclass covers devices and accessories designed to dry a person's body using these sheet-type materials within the sanitary equipment sector.
[A47K 10/22] for rolled-up webs -- The A47K 10/22 group covers body-drying implements that use rolled-up webs, such as dispensers or holders for paper towels, wet wipes or similar sheets. It includes devices that unwind a continuous roll to provide a length of material for drying the skin or hands.
[A47K 10/24] Towel dispensers -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[A47K 10/26] Mechanically-driven towel dispensers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanically-driven towel dispensers, e.g. with storing devices for soiled towels.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Dispensers for paper towels or toilet paper dispensing a clean part and taking-up a soiled part, e.g. using rolls -> A47K10/28 - Dispensers for paper towels or toilet paper dispensing from a web, e.g. with mechanical dispensing means -> A47K10/34 - Mechanical dispensers for paper towels or toilet paper dispensing from a store of single sheets, e.g. stacked -> A47K10/44
[A47K 10/28] dispensing a clean part and taking-up a soiled part, e.g. using rolls; -- A47K 10/28 relates to sanitary equipment such as hand-dryers or towel dispensers that provide a fresh section of the drying material while simultaneously collecting the used portion, often by means of a roll or similar mechanism. The classification covers devices that automate the dispensing of a clean part and the take-up of a soiled part within body-drying implements.
[A47K 10/30] combined with washing and drying means for the soiled parts -- A47K 10/30 covers sanitary furniture that incorporates both washing and drying functions for the parts that become soiled, such as towel racks, drying racks or other fixtures equipped with integrated washing and drying mechanisms for used linens or garments. It falls under Human Necessities → Furniture → Sanitary equipment → Body-drying implements.
[A47K 10/32] Dispensers for paper towels or toilet paper -- A47K 10/32 covers devices that hold and dispense paper towels or toilet paper as part of sanitary equipment. The class includes countertop or wall-mounted dispensers designed for hygienic hand-drying or bathroom use, providing controlled release of individual paper sheets.
[A47K 10/34] dispensing from a web, e.g. with mechanical dispensing means -- A47K 10/34 covers devices that feed a continuous web of absorbent material, such as paper towels or cloth strips, and dispense a portion for use, typically using a mechanical mechanism. These systems are used in sanitary furniture for body-drying purposes.
[A47K 10/36] with cutting devices -- A47K 10/36 covers body-drying implements that incorporate cutting devices, such as hair-dryers combined with electric shavers or towel dispensers equipped with scissors. The classification groups any sanitary equipment designed to dry a person while simultaneously providing a means to cut hair, nails or other material.
[A47K 10/3606] The cutting devices being motor driven -- A47K 10/3606 covers motor-driven cutting tools incorporated in devices for drying the body, such as electric
[A47K 10/3612] with drive and pinch rollers -- This subclass concerns body-drying implements equipped with a motor-driven system that uses pinch rollers to convey air or a drying medium onto the user. It covers hand or foot dryers where rollers guide airflow for efficient drying
[A47K 10/3618] The drive roller pressing against the paper roll -- A driven roller that contacts a paper roll to feed and dispense hand-drying towels or similar sheets, providing the mechanical force needed to unwind the roll in a sanitary body-drying device. This arrangement is used in towel dispensers integrated into hand-drying equipment.
[A47K 10/3625] with electronic control means -- The CPC subclass A47K 10/3625 covers body-drying appliances such as hand-dryers or personal dryers that incorporate electronic control means, including sensors, timers, variable-speed drives or programmable settings for activating and regulating the drying operation. It falls under Human Necessities → Furniture → Sanitary Equipment → Body-drying implements.
[A47K 10/3631] The cutting devices being driven manually -- A47K 10/3631 covers manually operated cutting tools incorporated in body-drying implements such as towel dispensers or bath-robe accessories. The classification pertains to devices where the user provides the force to cut, for example scissors or blade mechanisms attached to towels, wipes or similar sanitary items. It falls under the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Furniture → Sanitary Equipment → Body-drying implements.
[A47K 10/3637] using a crank or handle -- The A47K 10/3637 subclass groups body-drying appliances that are driven by a crank or handle, such as manually operated towel-wringers, hand-cranked hair or skin dryers, and similar devices where the user rotates a lever or handle to generate the drying action.
[A47K 10/3643] by pulling the paper -- A47K 10/3643 covers body-drying implements that dispense paper by means of a pulling action, such as pull-out or pull-tab paper towel dispensers used after washing. The classification relates to sanitary equipment within furniture for personal hygiene. It specifies mechanisms where the user pulls a sheet of paper to dry the body.
[A47K 10/3656] with paper jam preventing devices -- A47K 10/3656 covers body-drying implements such as hand dryers or paper-towel dispensers that incorporate mechanisms to stop paper from jamming, ensuring continuous operation and preventing blockage of the drying system.
[A47K 10/3662] with devices preventing the use of non-authorised paper rolls -- A47K 10/3662 covers body-drying implements such as hand dryers that incorporate mechanisms to block or limit the use of paper rolls that are not authorised, typically by detecting roll size, material or RFID tags and disabling operation when an unapproved roll is inserted. This subclass falls under sanitary equipment within furniture for human necessities.
[A47K 10/3687] with one or more reserve rolls -- A47K 10/3687 pertains to body-drying sanitary equipment that includes one or more reserve rolls of absorbent material. The reserve rolls are provided to maintain continuous drying operation when the primary roll is depleted.
[A47K 10/38] the web being rolled-up -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dispensers for paper towels or toilet paper dispensing from a web, the web being rolled up with or without a tearing edge.
[A47K 10/3809] with roll spindles which are not directly supported -- A47K 10/3809 classifies body-drying implements that use a roll of material whose spindle is mounted without direct support from the main housing or frame. The entry applies to devices such as hand-dryers or towel dispensers where the roll is suspended or guided by indirect mechanisms, distinguishing them from models with directly supported spindles.
[A47K 10/3818] with a distribution opening which is perpendicular to the rotation axis -- A body-drying device, such as a hand dryer, that features a distribution opening positioned at a right angle to the element's rotation axis, directing airflow perpendicular to the spinning part for efficient drying. This design falls under sanitary equipment in the furniture category.
[A47K 10/3827] with a distribution opening which is parallel to the rotation axis -- A47K 10/3827 classifies body-drying implements such as hand or hair dryers that feature an air-distribution opening aligned parallel to the rotation axis of the fan or blower, directing airflow along the same line as the rotating element. This design detail is used to control and streamline the drying airflow.
[A47K 10/3836] with roll spindles which are supported at one side -- A47K 10/3836 pertains to sanitary equipment in the body-drying implement category, specifically hand-dryers or similar devices that employ roll spindles which are mounted and supported on only one side. This feature relates to the mechanical arrangement of the drying element within the overall apparatus.
[A47K 10/3845] with devices preventing the use of non-authorised paper rolls -- A47K 10/3845 classifies body-drying devices such as hand dryers that incorporate a mechanism to prevent the insertion or use of paper rolls that are not authorised, typically by locking or detection systems that restrict operation to supplied consumables. This falls under the sanitary equipment group within the furniture category of the Human Necessities section.
[A47K 1/04] Basins -- Basins are sanitary furniture items that form part of wash-stands, serving as receptacles for holding water for personal hygiene or cleaning tasks. They are typically integrated into bathroom or kitchen fixtures and may be made of ceramic, metal, glass or composite materials. This entry falls under the Human Necessities > Furniture > Sanitary Equipment hierarchy.
[A47K 10/40] with extensible or collapsible roll supports -- A47K 10/40 covers body-drying implements such as towels or drying racks that incorporate roll supports which can be extended or collapsed for compact storage or adjustable use.
[A47K 10/405] the roll support comprising several arm sections -- A roll support comprising several arm sections is a component of body-drying equipment such as towel- or bath-robe rollers. The multiple arms hold a roll in place while allowing it to unwind smoothly, facilitating convenient drying after bathing.
[A47K 10/42] dispensing from a store of single sheets, e.g. stacked -- The A47K 10/42 subclass covers devices that dispense individual sheets from a stacked store, such as towel or paper dispensers used in sanitary equipment. It includes mechanisms that separate and present a single sheet at a time for user convenience.
[A47K 10/421] dispensing from the top of the dispenser  A47K10/44  takes precedence -- A47K 10/421 covers body-drying implements, such as towels or wipes, that are dispensed from the top of a dispenser. It falls under the hierarchy HUMAN NECESSITIES > FURNITURE > SANITARY EQUIPMENT > Body-drying implements. The broader subclass A47K 10/44 has priority over this more specific code.
[A47K 10/422] with means for urging the whole stack upwards towards the dispensing opening, e.g. a spring, a counterweight -- A47K 10/422 classifies towel or similar drying implement dispensers that incorporate a mechanism-such as a spring or counterweight-to push the entire stack upward toward the opening, ensuring continuous, self-advancing dispensing.
[A47K 10/423] with hold-down means riding on the top of the stack, e.g. a press plate with dispensing opening -- A47K 10/423 belongs to the sanitary-equipment group for body-drying implements and covers devices that incorporate a hold-down means positioned on the top of a stack, such as a press plate with a dispensing opening, used to keep items in place while allowing them to be dispensed.
[A47K 10/424] dispensing from the bottom part of the dispenser  A47K10/44  takes precedence -- A47K 10/424 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to body-drying implements, specifically those that dispense items from the bottom part of the dispenser. This classification is used for inventions where the dispensing mechanism is located at the lower section, though A47K 10/44 takes precedence when applicable.
[A47K 10/425] with means for urging the whole stack towards the dispensing opening, e.g. a weight plate -- A47K 10/425 covers towel or similar body-drying implement dispensers that include a device, such as a weight plate, which pushes the entire stack of items toward the dispensing opening to ensure consistent delivery. The classification falls under sanitary equipment for human necessities.
[A47K 10/426] dispensing from the front or sides of the dispenser  A47K10/424  and  A47K10/44  take precedence -- A47K 10/426 pertains to body-drying implements such as hand dryers where the product is dispensed from the front or the sides of the dispenser. The subclass falls under sanitary equipment in the furniture group of human necessities and is overridden by the broader subclasses A47K 10/424 and A47K 10/44.
[A47K 10/427] with means for urging the whole stack towards the dispensing opening, e.g. a spring -- A47K 10/427 covers body-drying implements, such as towel or tissue dispensers, that incorporate a device - for example a spring - to push the whole stack toward the dispensing opening. The claim is limited to the urging mechanism that advances the entire roll or sheet pack.
[A47K 10/44] with mechanical dispensing means -- A47K 10/44 covers body-drying implements such as towels or similar absorbent sheets that are provided by a mechanical dispensing system, for example automatic towel dispensers that release a single piece on demand. This subgroup relates to sanitary equipment designed to deliver drying material without manual pulling.
[A47K 10/46] with means for storing soiled towels -- A47K 10/46 covers sanitary furniture such as towel racks or dispensers that incorporate a feature for collecting or storing used towels. The classification falls under body-drying implements within the furniture and sanitary equipment group of the CPC.
[A47K 10/48] Drying by means of hot air -- The A47K 10/48 class covers body-drying implements that use heated air to remove moisture, including hair dryers, hand dryers and similar devices designed for personal hygiene. It specifies the method of drying by circulating hot air rather than absorbent or mechanical means.
[A47K 10/485] combined with a textile or paper towel dispenser -- A body-drying implement that is integrated with a dispenser for textile or paper towels, such as a hand dryer combined with a towel roll or sheet feeder, falls under CPC A47K 10/485. This subclass groups sanitary equipment where drying and towel supply are provided together.
[A47K 1/05] Holding devices for basins or jugs -- This CPC subclass covers devices attached to wash-stands that support or secure basins, jugs or similar containers, such as brackets, clamps or frames. It pertains to furniture elements used in bathrooms or washrooms for holding water vessels.
[A47K 1/06] Replaceable hygienic linings or casings for wash-basins -- A47K 1/06 relates to replaceable hygienic linings or casings for wash-basins, covering removable inserts that can be swapped out to keep the basin clean. It is a subcategory of sanitary equipment within the furniture segment of human necessities.
[A47K 1/08] Accessories for toilet tables, e.g. glass plates, supports therefor -- The A47K 1/08 entry covers accessories for toilet tables, such as glass plates and the supports that hold them, which are used in wash-stands and other sanitary furniture. This falls under the CPC hierarchy of Human Necessities → Furniture → Sanitary Equipment → Wash-stands.
[A47K 1/09] Holders for drinking glasses, tooth brushes, hair brushes, or the like -- This subclass covers devices that support or retain personal items such as drinking glasses, toothbrushes, hair brushes and similar objects, usually as part of bathroom or wash-stand furniture. It includes racks, slots, trays or other mounting structures designed for convenient storage and easy access.
[A47K 1/10] Detachable frames with hand basins mountable on baths -- It refers to detachable framing structures that support a hand basin and can be fitted onto a bathtub, allowing removable wash-stand arrangements. They are designed for easy installation and removal, often used in bathroom furniture systems.
[A47K 11/00] Closets without flushing -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[A47K 11/02] Dry closets, e.g. incinerator closets -- A47K 11/02 covers dry closets, including incinerator toilets, designed for waste disposal without water flushing. These devices fall under sanitary equipment in the furniture sub-group of human necessities and are used where conventional flushing systems are unavailable.
[A47K 11/023] Incinerator closets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toilets provided with any devices for directly consuming, e.g. by burning excreta, i.e. urine and faeces and/or deodorize relative emissions, irrespective of the type of heating means used.  Accessories, e.g. pre-treatment devices, specially adapted or arranged to these toilets.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Incinerators specially adapted for consuming animal litter -> F23G7/00 - Sewage, sludge or waste products from water treatment installations -> F23G7/001
[A47K 11/026] with continuous tubular film for receiving faeces -- This CPC subgroup pertains to non-flushing toilets or closets that use a continuous tubular film or liner to receive and contain faeces, typically as a replaceable or disposable element. It falls under sanitary equipment within the furniture category of human necessities.
[A47K 11/03] having means for adding powder, e.g. earth -- A47K 11/03 classifies non-flushing sanitary closets equipped with a mechanism for introducing a powder, such as earth, to aid solid waste disposal. It falls under the broader categories of human necessities, furniture and sanitary equipment, covering designs that replace water-based flushing with powder-based treatment.
[A47K 11/035] with chemical solvents -- A47K 11/035 classifies sanitary closets that operate without flushing and employ chemical solvents to treat or neutralize waste. It covers designs, components and methods where liquid or solid waste is managed by chemical action rather than water discharge, falling under furniture for human necessities.
[A47K 11/04] Room closets -- The code A47K 11/04 covers room closets, which are built-in or freestanding storage compartments integrated into interior spaces for holding clothing, linens, or other personal items. It falls under the sanitary equipment subgroup of furniture within human necessities.
[A47K 11/045] inflatable or with inflatable parts -- A47K 11/045 classifies non-flush sanitary closets that incorporate inflatable or partially inflatable elements, such as portable bladder-type receptacles, inflatable liners or balloon-formed cavities. The subclass covers designs where the waste-holding chamber expands for use and collapses for compact storage, enabling temporary or mobile sanitary facilities.
[A47K 11/06] Chamber-pots -- A47K 11/06 covers chamber-pots, portable waterless receptacles for collecting human waste, primarily used in homes before indoor flushing toilets were common. These devices serve as a basic sanitary fixture in closets or bedrooms where no plumbing
[A47K 11/08] Night cabinets or tables with closets or bidet equipment -- A47K 11/08 classifies night cabinets or bedside tables that incorporate a small closet, storage compartment, or integrated bidet fixture, combining furniture for sleeping areas with sanitary or storage functions. This includes designs where a nightstand provides a concealed closet or bidet unit without a flushing system.
[A47K 11/10] Hand tools for cleaning the toilet bowl -- A47K 11/10 covers hand-operated devices used to scrub or otherwise clean the interior of a toilet bowl,
[A47K 11/105] Disposable covers to keep the bowl clean -- A47K 11/105 covers disposable protective liners or covers designed to keep the bowl of a closet-type sanitary equipment clean, typically used in non-flushing toilet installations. The classification falls under furniture sanitary equipment within human necessities.
[A47K 11/12] Urinals without flushing -- Urinals that operate without a flushing mechanism are classified under A47K 11/12, situated within the hierarchy of human necessities, furniture, sanitary equipment and closets without flushing. These fixtures provide a low-maintenance solution for male waste disposal, often using a simple drain or evaporative system instead of conventional water-based flushing.
[A47K 1/12] Wash-basins attachable to sinks -- A47K 1/12 covers wash-basins that are designed to be attached to or integrated with sinks, forming a combined unit for personal hygiene. The classification includes both detachable and permanently mounted basins used in bathroom furniture, emphasizing the functional relationship between the basin and the sink.
[A47K 1/14] Stoppers for wash-basins, baths, sinks, or the like -- The CPC subclass A47K 1/14 covers devices that seal or close the drain openings of wash-basins, baths, sinks and similar sanitary fixtures. It includes the design, mechanism and material aspects of stoppers, plugs and related sealing elements used to retain water in these fixtures. This subclass falls within the hierarchy Human Necessities → Furniture → Sanitary Equipment → Wash-stands.
[A47K 13/00] Seats or covers for all kinds of closets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toilet seats or covers or accessories therefor, e.g. mounting, cleaning, heating or ventilating devices or user protections.
[A47K 13/005] Auxiliary or portable seats  for children  A47K13/06 -- A47K 13/005 covers auxiliary or portable seats designed for children that can be used inside or attached to closets and similar storage furniture. These devices provide temporary, often fold-away or detachable, seating to help children reach high shelves, dress, or sit safely while using the closet. The narrower subclass A47K 13/06 specifies portable child seats.
[A47K 13/02] of plastic materials -- A47K 13/02 includes seats and covers made of plastic for any type of closet, such as plastic chair pads, bench seats or protective liners intended for use inside wardrobes or storage cabinets.
[A47K 13/04] Buffers for seats -- A47K 13/04 covers devices that provide cushioning or protective padding for seats in closets, including pads, cushions or other buffer elements that enhance comfort or prevent wear on the seating surface.
[A47K 13/06] Auxiliary or portable seats for children -- A47K 13/06 covers auxiliary or portable seats specifically designed for children, such as booster seats, travel chairs, or fold-away stools used in closets or other furniture. These devices provide temporary seating for children and fall under sanitary equipment within the furniture category.
[A47K 13/08] Covers with urine funnels -- A47K 13/08 pertains to protective covers for closet seats that integrate a urine-funneling device, directing urine away from the seat to improve hygiene and simplify cleaning in sanitary furniture.
[A47K 13/10] Devices for raising and lowering -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[A47K 13/105] Toilet seat or toilet cover handles -- The CPC subclass A47K 13/105 covers handles designed for toilet seats or toilet covers, including any mechanisms that allow opening, closing, or lifting the seat or lid. It pertains to sanitary equipment within the broader group of furniture for human necessities.
[A47K 13/12] Hinges -- This subclass concerns hinges employed in closet furniture, including doors, seats and covers, and covers a range of hinge types used to attach and pivot these components. It is situated within the sanitary equipment field of the broader categories of human necessities, furniture and closet accessories.
[A47K 13/14] Protecting covers for closet seats -- This CPC subclass A47K 13/14 relates to protective coverings for seats incorporated in closets, such as bench or chair tops inside wardrobes, cupboards or other storage furniture. It includes designs, materials and attachment methods that shield the seat surface from wear, dirt or damage while the furniture remains in use.
[A47K 13/145] of rolled-up paper tubes or rolled-up plastic tubes -- A47K 13/145 refers to seats or covers for all types of closets, specifically those made from rolled-up paper tubes or rolled-up plastic tubes, under the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the EPO and USPTO.
[A47K 13/16] of single sheets of paper -- A47K 13/16 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to seats or covers for all types of closets, specifically addressing those made from single sheets of paper. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office to organize patents related to sanitary furniture.
[A47K 13/165] Dispensers therefor -- A47K 13/165 relates to devices that automatically or manually dispense seat covers, liners or similar protective items for furniture such as closet seats used in sanitary equipment. The classification covers mechanisms that store and release these accessories on demand.
[A47K 13/18] of paper webs -- This CPC subclass covers the use of paper webs as material for seats or covers on closet furniture, including paper liners, cushions or protective sheets that are placed on shelves, drawers or hanging rods.
[A47K 13/20] with cutting devices -- A47K 13/20 covers closet seats, lids or other protective coverings that integrate cutting tools, such as blades or scissors, for performing tasks like trimming, shaving or cutting while seated. The classification applies to furniture items designed for sanitary environments where a built-in cutter is incorporated into the seat or cover mechanism.
[A47K 13/22] rolled-up  -- A47K 13/22 covers rolled-up seats or covers that are fitted inside various types of closets, such as swing-out or pull-out wardrobe components. These flexible, collapsible elements are classified under sanitary equipment within the broader furniture group of human necessities. This CPC entry specifies the design and construction of such rolled-up accessories for storage units.
[A47K 13/225] with means for taking up the soiled part -- A47K 13/225 covers seats or covers fitted to wardrobes, closets or similar cupboards that incorporate a mechanism for collecting used or soiled items, such as a removable bin, tray or suction system that gathers waste directly from the sitting surface. This subclass relates to sanitary furniture designed to keep the interior of a closet clean while in use.
[A47K 13/24] Parts or details not covered in, or of interest apart from, groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. devices imparting a swinging or vibrating motion to the seats.
[A47K 13/242] Devices for locking the cover in the closed position -- A47K 13/242 relates to mechanisms that keep closet doors, drawers or other storage covers securely closed. It includes locks, latches, magnetic catches and similar devices designed to lock the cover in the closed position.
[A47K 13/245] Devices for locking the cover in the open position -- A47K 13/245 covers devices that keep closet doors, panels, or seat covers in an open position by means of locking mechanisms. The solutions may include latches, brackets, magnetic catches or other fixtures that prevent the cover from closing unintentionally. These are used in furniture such as wardrobes, cabinets or sanitary equipment.
[A47K 13/247] Covers with sliding wings or wings pivoting around a vertical axis -- A47K 13/247 covers closet or cabinet doors whose protective panels move by sliding or by wings that pivot around a vertical axis, allowing the cover to open horizontally or swing vertically. This design is classified under sanitary equipment within furniture, a subset of human necessities.
[A47K 13/26] Mounting devices for seats or covers -- Mounting devices for seats or covers are components such as brackets, hooks, fasteners or other fixtures that attach or support a seat or a covering inside a closet. They enable the secure installation, adjustment or removal of the seat or cover within the sanitary equipment of a furniture set.
[A47K 13/28] Adjustably-mounted seats or covers -- The A47K 13/28 subclass covers inventions that provide seats or protective covers mounted on, or within, closet-type sanitary units and that can be adjusted in position. It includes mechanisms for raising, lowering or tilting the seat or cover to improve user comfort or save space. This falls under Human Necessities → Furniture → Sanitary Equipment → Seats or covers for all kinds of closets.
[A47K 13/30] Seats having provisions for heating, deodorising or the like -- This subclass covers seats installed in closets such as wardrobes that incorporate heating elements, deodorising systems or similar features to provide comfort and odor control. It includes integrated heating pads, fragrance dispensers or ventilation for scented air inside the seat.
[A47K 13/302] Seats with cleaning devices -- A47K 13/302 designates seats that incorporate cleaning mechanisms, such as built-in vacuums, brushes or water-spraying systems, intended for use in closets or similar storage furniture. The classification applies to sanitary furniture where the seating element and its cleaning device are combined in a single unit.
[A47K 13/305] Seats with heating devices -- A47K 13/305 covers seats that incorporate a heating device, such as electric or other heat-generating elements, intended to provide warmth to the user. The classification applies to heated seating used in furniture like closet or cabinet seats and similar sanitary-equipment applications.

[A47K 17/00] Other sanitary equipment not covered by the other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Other equipment concerning the sanitary devices not otherwise provided in A47K , e.g.:
[A47K 17/003] Dispensers or holders for protecting covers for toilet bowls, seats or covers  combined with seats  A47K13/24 -- A47K 17/003 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to dispensers or holders designed for protecting covers for toilet bowls, seats, or lids, specifically those that are integrated with seats. This classification is part of a broader category covering other sanitary equipment not specified elsewhere in this subclass.
[A47K 17/02] Body supports, other than seats, for closets, e.g. handles, back-rests, foot-rests -- A47K 17/02 covers non-seating body supports designed for closets, such as handles, back-rests and foot-rests that aid the user while accessing or using a wardrobe. These elements are classified under sanitary equipment within the broader group of furniture for human necessities.
[A47K 17/022] Wall mounted grab bars or handles, with or without support on the floor -- A47K 17/022 covers wall-mounted grab bars or handles used in bathrooms or other sanitary settings, including designs that may also be supported by the floor. These devices assist users in maintaining balance or transitioning between sitting and standing positions. They fall under the sanitary equipment subclass of furniture.
[A47K 17/024] pivotally mounted on the wall -- This subclass covers sanitary equipment that is pivotally mounted on a wall, such as hinged washbasins or sinks, and includes other wall-mounted devices not classified in the remaining groups of the sanitary equipment subclass.
[A47K 17/026] Armrests mounted on or around the toilet  A47K17/022  takes precedence -- A47K 17/026 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to armrests specifically designed for mounting on or around toilets, with a note that A47K 17/022 takes precedence if applicable. This classification is part of the broader category of other sanitary equipment not covered by more specific groups within the furniture and human necessities categories.
[A47K 17/028] Foot- or knee-rests -- A47K 17/028 covers foot- or knee-rests designed as part of sanitary equipment, such as bathroom or toilet furniture. These devices support the feet or knees to improve comfort and hygiene while using sanitary fixtures, and are classified under the broader categories of furniture and human necessities.
[A47K 2003/305] Sealings between screen and bath- or showertub -- A47K 2003/305 covers sealing arrangements that join a screen, such as a filter or door, to a bath or shower tub to prevent fluid leakage. The entry applies to appliances like dishwashers and washing machines where a watertight connection between the screen and tub is required.
[A47K 2003/307] Adjustable connections to the wall -- A47K 2003/307 covers devices that provide a variable or adjustable means of securing kitchen furniture or appliances to a wall, such as brackets, rails, sliding clips or mounting frames that can be repositioned to accommodate leveling, alignment or remodeling.
[A47K 2003/365] Liftable articulated screens to be swung over the faucets and against the wall -- A47K 2003/365 covers kitchen equipment in which a liftable, articulated screen can be swung outward from a countertop, positioned over the faucets, and then folded back against the wall for storage, providing a flexible splash guard or work surface. This design combines a hinged or telescoping mechanism with a movable panel to protect the surrounding area while allowing easy access when needed.
[A47K 2003/367] Hinges urging the articulated screen in one or more stable positions -- A47K 2003/367 refers to hinges designed to keep an articulated screen, such as a tablet or laptop display, locked in one or more predetermined positions. The classification covers mechanisms that allow the screen to be set and remain stable without movement, typically using friction, detents, or locking elements. It applies to devices where the hinge provides reliable positioning for viewing or operation.
[A47K 2005/1218] Table mounted;  Dispensers integrated with the mixing tap -- A table-mounted bathroom tap that incorporates one or more dispensing units, such as soap, sanitizer or other liquids, directly into the mixing faucet assembly. The classification covers designs where the dispenser is integrated into the tap body and mounted on a countertop or table surface.
[A47K 2010/3206] Coreless paper rolls -- A47K 2010/3206 covers paper rolls that are supplied without a rigid core, being wound onto a removable sleeve or directly onto a machine for later unwinding. These coreless rolls are used in industrial printing, packaging and converting processes where a core would interfere with material handling.
[A47K 2010/3213] activated by coins -- A47K 2010/3213 classifies coin-activated food or beverage dispensing apparatus, such as vending machines and snack dispensers that start operation when a coin is inserted. It falls under the broader A47K group covering tableware, kitchenware and related household equipment.
[A47K 2010/322] with means for deodorizing the ambient air -- A47K 2010/322 classifies cooking appliances that incorporate built-in means for deodorising the surrounding air, such as filters, adsorbents or ionising devices that reduce odors emitted during use. This subgroup covers any kitchen equipment where air-cleaning functionality is integrated with the primary cooking function.
[A47K 2010/3226] collecting data of usage -- A47K 2010/3226 relates to technologies that gather usage information from food-preparation, beverage-dispensing or kitchen equipment, such as sensors, counters or networked interfaces that record operation cycles, quantities dispensed or user interactions. The code is applied to patents describing data-collection, monitoring or analytics functions connected with such appliances.
[A47K 2010/3233] Details of the housing, e.g. hinges, connection to the wall -- A47K 2010/3233 refers to the part of the cooperative patent classification that deals with the structural elements of a kitchen or food-preparation unit's housing, including features such as hinges, mounting brackets and methods for attaching the unit to a wall. It classifies inventions that address how the enclosure is connected, opened or secured within a kitchen setting.
[A47K 2010/324] Jumbo rolls -- A47K 2010/324 classifies inventions relating to jumbo rolls, i.e., oversized bakery products such as large bread rolls or similar baked goods. It covers methods of producing, shaping, baking, and packaging these large rolls, as well as equipment specially designed for their manufacture or handling. The entry falls under the CPC group for tableware and kitchen utensils concerning food preparation and serving items.
[A47K 2010/3246] Locking mechanisms for the housing -- A47K concerns domestic appliances such as cooking and food-preparation equipment. The sub-group 2010/3246 relates to locking mechanisms for the housing of these appliances, including safety interlocks, door or cover locks that prevent operation when the enclosure is open.
[A47K 2010/3253] with one or more reserve rolls -- A47K 2010/3253 classifies coffee, tea or cocoa dispensing devices that incorporate one or more reserve rolls, which hold extra product to feed the main dispensing mechanism and enable uninterrupted service. This subgroup is used for machines where a supplemental roll supplies material when the primary feed is depleted.
[A47K 2010/326] dispensing simultaneously from several rolls or stacks -- A47K 2010/326 relates to devices or methods that dispense material from two or more rolls or stacks simultaneously, such as parallel-feed dispensers for towels, napkins or similar sheet products.

The classification groups products whose primary function is activated by wetting, ensuring they are used in a damp state for optimal performance.

The classification includes mechanisms for delivering heat to the wipe and may specify power sources, temperature control, or safety features.

This classification is shared by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office for harmonized patent documentation.

[A47K 2010/3681] characterised by the way a new paper roll is loaded in the dispenser -- The invention concerns a beverage or food dispenser in which a new paper roll-such as a filter-paper cartridge for a coffee or tea machine-is introduced by a specific loading arrangement that secures the roll, aligns it with the dispensing mechanism, and enables easy replacement without dismantling the main unit.

[A47K 2010/3863] with roll rotation braking devices -- A47K 2010/3863 covers food-processing equipment in which a roll or cylinder is driven to rotate for shaping, sheeting or laminating dough, pastry or similar materials, and includes a braking device that can control, slow or stop the roll's rotation. [A47K 5/08] for solid soap -- This subgroup of the CPC covers devices that hold or dispense solid soap, such as soap dishes, trays or built-in dispensers used in bathroom or kitchen fixtures. It is classified under the sanitary equipment branch of furniture within human necessities.
[A47K 5/09] with means for scraping or grating -- This subclass covers holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste or similar substances that include a built-in scraper or grater to remove remaining product from the container or the dispensing surface.
[A47K 5/10] for powdered soap -- A47K 5/10 covers devices that hold or dispense powdered soap, including similar dispensers for toothpaste or comparable personal-care pastes. The entry falls under the sanitary equipment subgroup of furniture, specifying mechanisms that store the powder and release measured amounts for use.
[A47K 5/12] for liquid or pasty soap -- A47K 5/12 covers devices that dispense liquid or pasty soap, such as pump bottles and wall-mounted soap dispensers used in bathrooms or kitchens. The subclass includes mechanisms that release controlled amounts of soap from containers integrated into sanitary equipment.
[A47K 5/1201] hand-carried -- A47K 5/1201 classifies hand-carried holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste and similar personal-care products. These portable units are designed to be held in the hand while dispensing the substance.
[A47K 5/1202] dispensing dosed volume  A47K5/1201  takes precedence -- A47K 5/1202 covers holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste or similar products that deliver a pre-measured dose of the material. It is a sub-category of A47K 5/1201, which defines the broader class of dispensing devices for sanitary household items.
[A47K 5/1204] by means of a rigid dispensing chamber and pistons -- This class includes holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste or similar substances that work by means of a rigid, non-flexible dispensing chamber combined with one or more pistons to release the product.
[A47K 5/1205] Dispensing from the top of the dispenser with a vertical piston -- A47K 5/1205 covers holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste or similar products that release the material from the top of the dispenser by means of a vertically moving piston, falling within the sanitary equipment section of the furniture class.
[A47K 5/1207] Dispensing from the bottom of the dispenser with a vertical piston -- The subclass A47K 5/1207 covers holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste or similar products that release the material from the bottom of the container using a vertically moving piston. The piston pushes the product upward so it exits through a bottom outlet, a layout common in many sanitary dispensing devices.
[A47K 5/1208] by means of a flexible dispensing chamber -- This subclass of the CPC for furniture sanitary equipment deals with holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste and similar products that operate by means of a flexible dispensing chamber, allowing controlled release when the chamber is squeezed or deformed.
[A47K 5/1209] with chamber in the form of a cylindrical tube -- A47K 5/1209 classifies sanitary dispensers-such as soap or toothpaste holders-that incorporate a chamber shaped as a cylindrical tube for storing the product. It belongs to the furniture group within human necessities, specifically to holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste or similar items.
[A47K 5/1211] using pressure on soap, e.g. with piston  A47K5/1201  takes precedence; using squeeze bottles or the like  A47K5/122 -- A47K 5/1211 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste, or similar products that operate by applying pressure on the substance, typically through a piston mechanism. This code is specific to devices where direct mechanical pressure facilitates the dispensing process, distinguishing it from other methods like squeeze bottles.
[A47K 5/1212] applied by a screwing action -- A47K 5/1212 refers to holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste or similar products that are mounted by means of a screwing action. The subclass includes designs where the dispenser is attached to a wall, countertop or fixture through threaded connections, allowing secure and adjustable installation.
[A47K 5/1214] applied by gravitational force -- A47K 5/1214 covers holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste or similar products that release the material by gravity rather than by pumping or squeezing. The classification applies to devices where the product flows downward from a reservoir when the container is tilted or inverted. It is used for sanitary equipment within the furniture subclass of human necessities.
[A47K 5/1215] applied by a peristaltic action -- A47K 5/1215 covers holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste or similar substances that operate by a peristaltic action, using a wave-like compression to push product out of the container. These devices are classified under sanitary equipment within furniture for human necessities.
[A47K 5/1217] Electrical control means for the dispensing mechanism -- A47K 5/1217 concerns the electrical control means used in dispensers for soap, toothpaste and similar products. It includes sensors, switches, timers or electronic circuits that activate or regulate the dispensing mechanism.
[A47K 5/122] using squeeze bottles or the like -- A47K 5/122 covers the use of squeeze bottles or similar containers to dispense products such as soap, toothpaste or cosmetics from holders or dispensers. It falls under the sanitary-equipment group of furniture, specifying mechanisms that rely on manual pressure to expel the material.
[A47K 5/13] of invertible type -- A47K 5/13 covers holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste or similar substances that are of an invertible type, meaning the
[A47K 5/14] Foam or lather making devices -- A47K 5/14 covers devices that turn liquid soap, toothpaste or similar products into foam or lather as they are dispensed. The classification includes mechanisms such as pump heads, aerators and mixing chambers that add air to the product during use, typically integrated into bathroom or kitchen dispensers.
[A47K 5/16] with mechanical drive -- A47K 5/16 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to holders or dispensers for soap, toothpaste, or similar items that feature a mechanical drive mechanism. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office to organize patents related to sanitary equipment, specifically those with functional mechanical components for dispensing products.
[A47K 5/18] for both soap and toothpaste or the like -- A47K 5/18 defines holders or dispensers that can deliver both soap and toothpaste or similar hygiene products, intended for use in furniture-related sanitary equipment such as bathroom or kitchen fixtures. It covers combined dispensing devices that serve multiple cleaning or oral-care substances in a single unit.

[A47L] DOMESTIC WASHING OR CLEANING -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The following overview of the main groups indicating the coverage of the subclass A47L is given below.  A detailed description of the covered subject matter at main-group level, e.g. A47L15/00 , is to be found at said main groups.  Cleaning windows, window shades, window screens, venetian blinds, Safety devices for use in window cleaning  Suction cleaners  Machines and implements for cleaning floors, carpets, furniture, walls, or wall coverings:  Apparatus or implements for cleaning or polishing household articles or the like:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Brushes -> A46B - Laundry -> D06F
[A47L 1/00] Cleaning windows, window shades, window screens, venetian blinds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All aspects of cleaning windows including wetting, cleaning and drying of the window surface.  Power-driven machines or devices for cleaning windows, e.g. all machines and devices with a power driven motor, power heating element or the like.  Hand implements for cleaning windows, e.g. all hand operated implements.
[A47L 1/02] Power-driven machines or devices -- A47L 1/02 covers power-driven machines and devices used for cleaning windows, window shades, window screens and venetian blinds. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Human Necessities - Furniture - Domestic washing or cleaning. The code applies to technologies such as motorized squeegees, robotic cleaners and powered brush systems for glass and shading surfaces.

[A47L 1/06] Hand implements -- A47L 1/06 designates hand-operated tools for cleaning windows, window shades, screens and venetian blinds, including items such as squeegees, wipers, cloths and small brushes. It is nested within Human necessities > Furniture > Domestic washing or cleaning and groups together manual implements used to maintain glass surfaces and related fittings.
[A47L 1/08] with provision for supplying liquids, e.g. cleaning agents -- A47L 1/08 covers domestic cleaning devices for windows, window shades, screens or venetian blinds that incorporate a system for supplying cleaning liquids, such as built-in reservoirs or spray mechanisms, enabling the user to apply agents while wiping or washing the surface. This subclass falls under the hierarchy Human necessities → Furniture → Domestic washing or cleaning.
[A47L 1/09] for cleaning one side with access from the other side only -- A47L 1/09 covers devices or methods for cleaning the surface of a window, screen, shade or Venetian blind when only the opposite side can be reached, enabling removal of dirt or deposits from one side without direct access to the cleaned side.

[A47L 11/00] Cleaning floors, carpets, furniture, walls, or wall coverings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Floor surfacing or polishing machines. ( A47L11/02 - A47L11/20 )  In this group polishing (burnishing, buffering, waxing) : to provide a glossy, reflective surface. Can be aided with polishing agents or waxes.  Floor-sweeping machines. ( A47L11/22 - A47L11/24 )  In this group sweeping is defined as a dry (in contrast to scrubbing) operation involving removing dust, debris and larger particles from a floor such as brushing, vacuuming or blowing - i.e. to loose particles.  Floor-scrubbing machines ( A47L11/26 - A47L11/307 )  In this group scrubbing (scouring): wet operation involving application of water/cleaning composition together with mechanical action on the floor surface with mops, rotating pads or brushes or other cleaning tools.  It may involve recovery of the dirty liquid or not.  Carpet-sweepers. ( A47L11/32 - A47L11/33 )  In this group a carpet sweeper in the sense of A47L11/32 and subgroups is defined as a mechanical device for the cleaning of carpets that typically consists of a small box, the base of the box having rollers and brushes, connected by a belt or gears. There may also be a container for dirt.  Machines for treating carpets in position by liquid, foam, or vapour, e.g. by steam. ( A47L11/34 )  These machines are intended for powerful carpet extraction - i.e. for removing deep, embedded soils in carpets laying on the floor (also called carpet extractors).  Higher pressure steam, cleaning fluid and/or shampoo is applied to the carpet. Scrubbing action may be used (brushes, etc...).  Dirty liquid is normally - not always - recovered by the machine.  Machines for beating upholstery ( A47L11/36 ).  Machines, specially adapted for cleaning walls, ceilings, roofs, or the like ( A47L11/38 ).  Parts or details of the machines, e.g. handles, arrangement of switches, skirts,... ( A47L11/40 - A47L11/4097 ).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Vacuum-cleaners -> A47L5/00 ; A47L7/00 ; A47L9/00 - Floor, walls, ceilings sanding or grinding machines -> B24B7/18

[A47L 13/00] Implements for cleaning floors, carpets, furniture, walls, or wall coverings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Scraping implements such as steel wool, wire meshes...  Scrubbing implements such as wipes, sponges, cloths, mops...  Scouring implements including scouring implements for hearths or metal objects.  Polishing implements as well as squeegees, carpet beaters, dusters and other implements for cleaning floors, carpets, furniture, walls or wall coverings in the domestic ambit.  Manual operation of the user is required to perform the cleaning action.  Auxiliary devices related to said implements are classified here too (storing devices, shakers for dust-cloths, dust pans, crumb trays, wringers, kneeling supports for cleaning floors...).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Implements used in manual washing or polishing of crockery, table-ware or the like -> A47L17/00 ; A47L21/00 - Dusting implements using adhesive surfaces -> A47L25/005 - Brooms -> A46B

[A47L 13/58] Wringers for scouring pads, mops, or the like, combined with buckets -- A47L 13/58 covers devices that combine a bucket with a wringer for scouring pads, mops or similar cleaning tools, enabling water to be expelled from the pad while it remains attached to the bucket.
[A47L 13/59] with movable squeezing members -- This subclass covers cleaning implements for floors, carpets, furniture, walls or wall coverings that incorporate movable squeezing members, such as compressible rollers, squeegees or other deformable elements used to expel or apply liquid during operation.
[A47L 13/60] with squeezing rollers -- A47L 13/60 covers floor-cleaning implements that incorporate squeezing rollers to extract water or moisture from a mop, pad or cleaning head during use, allowing the user to wring out the tool without separate manual effort. The feature is applied to devices for cleaning floors, carpets, furniture or walls, enhancing efficiency and ergonomics in domestic washing or cleaning tasks.
[A47L 13/62] Kneeling supports, especially wheeled -- A47L 13/62 covers kneeling supports intended for use while cleaning floors, carpets, furniture, walls or wall coverings, especially those equipped with wheels to facilitate movement. These devices help users maintain a low-posture position during cleaning tasks.
[A47L 15/00] Cleaning or polishing household articles or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Features of washing or rinsing machines for crockery or tableware, i.e. machines which are suitable for washing or rinsing articles such as dishes, plates, knives, forks, spoons, glasses, cups, etc., used at meals or in the kitchen, e.g. for cooking.  **Glossary:** - Dishwashing machine: a machine for washing dishes, plates and like tableware, cutlery, cooking vessels and utensils.
[A47L 15/0002] Washing processes, i.e. machine working principles characterised by phases or operational steps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Washing methods or processes, i.e. machine working principles or operational steps.  In the field of washing machine for crockery and/or tableware there exists a clear distinction between washing methods and washing processes.  A washing method defines the washing principle according to which a machine works: by utilising water, ultrasonic wave, microwaves, steam, etc.  A washing process defines the series of operations performed by a machine to wash the tableware according to a defined washing principle.  For example, a conventional domestic dishwasher uses water for washing the crockery and the washing processes of domestic dishwashers usually comprise the series of operations of: rinsing with cold water, washing with water containing detergent and rinsing with hot water.  This distinction between washing methods and washing processes is not always used in documents in the field of dishwashers.  However, a clear indication that a dishwasher application relates to a washing method and/or process, is given by the subject-matter of the application, which must relate to the way the crockery or the tableware is treated.  Does the invention information of the application concern the principle according to which the dishes are treated? Is steam applied to the crockery? Are microwaves used for drying the tableware? Then there is a clear indication that the technical information of the application relates to a washing method.  Does the invention information concern the temporal sequence of the machine operational steps affecting the washing cycle of the dishes? Pre-rinse, washing, final rinse and drying? Does the invention information concern the values of the washing parameters affecting the crockery? What will be the value of the water temperature during the rinsing step? What will be the value of the air temperature during the drying step? Then there is a clear indication that the application relates to a washing process.
[A47L 15/0005] Rinsing phases, e.g. pre-rinsing, intermediate rinsing, final rinsing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Applications characterized by temporal sequence of rinsing operations and/or rinsing parameters (e.g. water temperature, pressure) affecting the crockery during a rinsing phase.
[A47L 15/0007] Washing phases -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Applications characterized by temporal sequence of washing operations and/or washing parameters (e.g. water temperature, pressure) affecting the crockery during a washing phase.
[A47L 15/001] Drain phases, including initial, intermediate or partial draining phases -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Applications characterized by temporal sequence of draining operations and/or draining parameters (e.g. partial draining) during a washing cycle.
[A47L 15/0013] Drying phases, including dripping-off phases -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Applications characterized by temporal sequence of drying operations and/or drying parameters (e.g. temperature of air) affecting the crockery during a drying phase.
[A47L 15/0015] other treatment phases, e.g. steam or sterilizing phase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Applications characterized by temporal sequence and/or parameters of other particular treatments affecting the crockery during a washing cycle.
[A47L 15/0018] Controlling processes, i.e. processes to control the operation of the machine characterised by the purpose or target of the control  for control of water softening or water softener regeneration  A47L15/4229 ; for control of water level  A47L15/4244 ; for control of bad smells or odours  A47L15/4276 ; for control of water temperature  A47L15/4287 ; for control of water pressure  A47L15/4289 ; for control of condition of crockery or tableware  A47L15/4295 ; for control of condition of washing water  A47L15/4297 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling method relating to the command of the machine, i.e. the control of any kind of operation of the machine. In the controlling methods information (manual or automatic inputs) is used by controlling means (e.g. a processor) to command actuators (e.g. valves, pump's motors, heating means, etc.)  A controlling method may have different purposes, for example: the control of a washing phase of a washing process (e.g. the control of the drying phase); the prevention of the malfunction of the machine; the control of a component of the machine (e.g. the detergent dispenser, the circulation pump, etc.).  The following picture schematically illustrates the workflow of a controlling method: Information (manual settings or automatic detections from e.g. sensors) is inputted into the controlling means which, depending on the purpose of the controlling, sends command signals to the dishwasher components.
[A47L 15/0021] Regulation of operational steps within the washing processes, e.g. optimisation or improvement of operational steps depending from the detergent nature or from the condition of the crockery -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling methods of the dishwashers operational steps. In other words, the control of the operation of the machine during a particular phase of the washing process.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Washing methods or processes -> A47L15/0002
[A47L 15/0023] Water filling  A47L15/4244  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling methods of any water filling phase in a dishwasher.  In other words, the purpose of the controlling method is to control the amount of water to let into the dishwasher and/or the length (time duration) of the water filling phase.  The main focus of the control is the particular operational step of the machine, i.e. the filling phase.  Such controlling methods may, for example, involve the monitoring of the water level in the dishwasher's tub to decide on when to terminate the water filling phase.  Other methods may, for example, involve the steps of operating a recirculation pump during the filling phase and monitoring the behaviour of the pump's electrical parameters in order to decide when to close the water inlet valve.

[A47L 15/0076] of non-domestic use type, e.g. commercial dishwashers for bars, hotels, restaurants, canteens or hospitals  machines specially adapted for drinking glasses  A47L15/0065 ; drawer-type dishwashers  A47L15/0084 ; in-sink dishwashers  A47L15/0086 ; miniature dishwashers  A47L15/0089 ; conveyor-type dishwashers  A47L15/24 ; machines with crockery cleaned by brushes  A47L15/37 -- **Glossary:** - Commercial dishwasher, domestic dishwasher: Domestic and commercial dishwashers differ substantially in design and manner of use, with the possible exception of a few commercial machine styles which are an outgrowth of domestic unit designs. Even though these latter designs may appear similar in many respects, they differ in function and operation, not only in the length of time it takes to wash and rinse a rack of ware, but also in the number of washes and rinses per rack.Domestic units ordinarily have the further capability of drying washed dishes within the wash chamber, whereas comparable commercial units may feature air drying outside the chamber in order to minimize the time it takes to complete each rack of ware. It is important in a commercial environment to wash racks successively in rapid order. Thus, a commercial washer may be used for washing and rinsing but not for drying, if the length of time it takes for the latter is too much.

[A47L 15/10] by introducing compressed air or other gas into the liquid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dishwashers usually comprising a vessel filled with water to a predetermined level and a compressed air arrangement by means of which air is introduced into the washing chamber. As the air enters the washing chamber it forms bubbles which flow across the articles and turbulate the water within the chamber.
[A47L 15/12] by a boiling effect -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dishwashers usually comprising a vessel filled with water to a predetermined level and heating means to heat the water up to the boiling point.  Alternatively the water can be heated in a separated boiler and once the water is boiling, the water is discharged over the dishes.
[A47L 15/13] using sonic or ultrasonic waves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dishwashers having sonic or ultrasonic generators connected to the water bath of a cleaning chamber.  In such dishwashers using for example ultrasonic generators, a high frequency sound wave is radiated by a generator through the water bath. This rapidly alternating wave produces a sequence of localized positive and negative pressure regions. The negative pressure regions effectively separate or cavitate the water bath to produce a large quantity of minute bubbles. As these cavitation bubbles within the water bath are contacted against a surface of a crockery item to be cleaned, the bubbles implode and thus provide a substantial mechanical scrubbing action which can be effective in dislodging and removing debris from the submerged crockery item.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Methods for cleaning crockery using sonic or ultrasonic waves -> A47L15/0002 , A47L2601/17 - Dish washers specially adapted for drinking glasses using sonic or ultrasonic waves -> A47L15/0065 , A47L2601/17 - Dish washers of non-domestic use type using sonic or ultrasonic waves -> A47L15/0076 , A47L2601/17 - Dish washers of the drawer-type using sonic or ultrasonic waves -> A47L15/0084 , A47L2601/17 - In-sink dishwashers using sonic or ultrasonic waves -> A47L15/0086 , A47L2601/17 - Dish washers of small size, e.g. portable mini dishwashers for small kitchens, using sonic or ultrasonic waves -> A47L15/0089 , A47L2601/17 - Dish washers specially adapted to wash large items like pots, using sonic or ultrasonic waves -> A47L15/0092 , A47L2601/17 - Dish washers specially adapted to wash large items like pots, using sonic or ultrasonic waves -> A47L15/0094 , A47L2601/17 - Dish washers with stationary crockery baskets and spraying devices within the cleaning chamber, using sonic or ultrasonic waves -> A47L15/14 , A47L2601/17 - Dish washers with movement of the crockery baskets by conveyors using sonic or ultrasonic waves -> A47L15/24 , A47L2601/17 - Dish washers with movement of the crockery baskets by other means, using sonic or ultrasonic waves -> A47L15/26 , A47L2601/17 - Dish washers with crockery cleaned by brushes using sonic or ultrasonic waves -> A47L15/37 , A47L2601/17

[A47L 15/4291] Recovery arrangements, e.g. for the recovery of energy or water -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dishwashers with heat recovery arrangements, i.e. dishwashers comprising heat exchangers and/or heat pump arrangements in process water circuits for the purpose of storing and/or recovering thermal energy.  For example, in this subgroup are classified dishwashers comprising heat transfer arrangements for transferring thermal energy from the circulating (or waste) water to the feed water:  An other example of dishwashers classified in this subgroup are dishwashers with water recovery arrangements, i.e. dishwashers comprising liquid storage container(s) for storing liquid (used in a previous wash/rinse phase of the wash cycle) in order to be reused subsequently:  The water storage tanks may or may not be arranged in heat-exchange relation with the washing tub and/or the hydraulic circuit.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Control of energy or water consumption -> A47L15/0047 - Heat pump arrangements during the drying phases -> A47L15/483

[A47L 17/00] Apparatus or implements used in manual washing or cleaning of crockery, table-ware, cooking-ware or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus or implements used in manual washing or cleaning of crockery, table-ware, cooking-ware or the like.  Manual operation of the user is required for performing the cleaning action.  The items to be cleaned can be crockery or table-ware (which can also usually be cleaned by dishwashing machines), but also bigger items such as oven plates.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Brushes for cleaning bottles -> A46B
[A47L 17/02] Basins -- Basins are shallow containers designed to hold water for manually washing or cleaning crockery, table-ware, cooking-ware and similar items, typically positioned on countertops or within a sink area. They belong to the domestic washing and cleaning segment of furniture intended for everyday human necessities.
[A47L 17/04] Pan or pot cleaning utensils -- A47L 17/04 covers manual utensils used to clean pans, pots and similar cookware. The classification includes brushes, scrubbers, sponges and other hand-operated tools designed for removing food residues from cooking vessels during domestic washing.
[A47L 17/06] Scrapers -- A47L 17/06 covers scrapers that are employed for manually washing or cleaning crockery, table-ware, cooking-ware and similar items. The class falls under Human Necessities → Furniture → Domestic Washing or Cleaning, focusing on hand-held tools for removing food residues and polishing dishes.
[A47L 17/08] Pads -- Pads are replaceable or reusable cleaning accessories designed to be attached to or used with hand-held washing tools for scrubbing dishes, tableware and cookware. They are typically made of cellulose, synthetic fibers or non-woven materials and may be impregnated with detergents or scouring agents to aid manual cleaning.
[A47L 17/10] Tongs for crockery -- A47L 17/10 covers hand-held tongs designed specifically for handling crockery, tableware or cooking-ware during manual washing or cleaning. These implements are classified under furniture for domestic washing and cleaning, facilitating safe removal and placement of dishes without direct hand contact.
[A47L 19/00] Drying devices for crockery or table-ware, e.g. tea-cloths -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Drying devices for crockery or table-ware. Their degree of complexity ranges from a draining board or rack where the items are placed and dried by letting the water drip to more complex ones involving an air circulation.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Drying arrangements for dishwashers -> A47L15/48 - Racks of dishwashers -> A47L15/50

[A47L 19/04] Crockery baskets -- A47L 19/04 classifies crockery baskets, which are containers-often made of wire, plastic or textile-used to hold plates, cups and other table-ware during washing, drying or storage. These devices form part of domestic washing or cleaning equipment within the furniture category of human necessities.
[A47L 21/00] Polishing of table-ware, e.g. knives, forks, spoons -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Implements, machines and supports or rests used for polishing of table-ware.  **Glossary:** - Polishing: Polishing involves a treatment of the surface e.g. of table-ware items to achieve a glossy surface.
[A47L 21/02] Machines -- A47L 21/02 covers machines that polish table-ware such as knives, forks and spoons, typically used in domestic washing or cleaning. The devices employ mechanical or abrasive actions to restore shine and smoothness to cutlery and other dining implements.
[A47L 21/04] Hand implements -- A47L 21/04 covers hand implements designed for polishing table-ware such as knives, forks and spoons during domestic washing or cleaning. It falls under the Human Necessities group, within Furniture and specifically the Domestic Washing or Cleaning subclass, addressing manual tools used to achieve a shine on cutlery.
[A47L 21/06] Supports or rests for use while polishing -- A47L 21/06 covers devices that hold or support table-ware items while they are being polished, such as rests for knives, forks and spoons, enabling hands-free polishing.
[A47L 2201/00] Robotic cleaning machines, i.e. with automatic control of the travelling movement or the cleaning operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This entry - A47L2201/00 - is an orthogonal code identifying aspects or details of robotic or autonomous suction cleaners ( A47L5/00 ; A47L7/00 ; A47L9/00 ) or cleaning machines ( A47L11/00 ) for cleaning floors, carpets, furniture, walls, or wall coverings, which are particular of robotic cleaners. This entry - A47L2201/00 - comprises inventive as well as additional information, covering in particular:  Docking stations: Static station where the autonomous cleaner is serviced for further cleaning - i.e. recharging of batteries, emptying dust container, exchange of cleaning brushes,...  Automatic control of the travelling movement: Navigation system, automatic obstacle detection,...  Control of the cleaning action for autonomous devices: Automatic detection of the surface type and application of the corresponding cleaning action (sweeping, scrubbing, vacuuming,...).
[A47L 2201/02] Docking stations -- Docking stations are fixtures that receive and secure robotic cleaning machines when they are idle, often providing power for recharging, data transfer, and sometimes routine maintenance. They are considered a type of domestic cleaning furniture that supports automatic operation by ensuring the robot is ready for the next cleaning cycle.
[A47L 2201/022] Recharging of batteries -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for recharging the batteries of autonomous cleaning robots, including charging stations, docking mechanisms and power-transfer systems integrated into domestic washing or cleaning equipment. It relates to robotic cleaning machines with automatic control of movement or cleaning operation.
[A47L 2201/024] Emptying dust or waste liquid containers -- The CPC subclass A47L 2201/024 deals with robotic cleaning machines that automatically control their travel and cleaning actions, focusing on the mechanisms for emptying dust or waste-liquid containers. It falls under Human Necessities → Furniture → Domestic washing or cleaning → Robotic cleaning machines.
[A47L 2201/026] Refilling cleaning liquid containers -- The subclass concerns devices or methods for replenishing the liquid supply in robotic cleaning machines such as autonomous vacuum or floor cleaners, allowing the cleaning-fluid reservoir to be refilled either automatically or manually during use. It falls under domestic washing or cleaning equipment within the furniture group of human necessities.
[A47L 2201/028] Refurbishing floor engaging tools, e.g. cleaning of beating brushes -- The A47L 2201/028 subclass deals with the refurbishment of floor-engaging components in robotic cleaning machines, focusing on the cleaning and maintenance of items such as beating brushes. It encompasses methods and apparatuses that restore brush performance and remove accumulated debris to keep the cleaning tool effective.
[A47L 2201/04] Automatic control of the travelling movement -- This subclass pertains to robotic cleaning machines that incorporate automatic control of their travelling movement. It covers systems that autonomously navigate floors or surfaces while performing cleaning tasks, without manual steering. The classification is used for inventions related to the motion control of such domestic cleaning robots.
[A47L 2201/06] Control of the cleaning action for autonomous devices -- A47L 2201/06 relates to the control of the cleaning action in autonomous domestic cleaning devices such as robot vacuums, covering methods and systems that automatically regulate travelling paths, brush speed, suction power or other cleaning parameters.

It includes any user-entered information, such as selected programmes, temperature or timing settings, that directs the dishwasher's operation. It includes all non-standard manual commands that influence the machine's cleaning method beyond the basic start/stop function.

The classification includes any apparatus that automates footwear cleaning, with or without integrated polishing functions.
[A47L 23/04] Hand implements for shoe-cleaning, with or without applicators for shoe polish -- A47L 23/04 covers manually operated tools designed specifically for cleaning shoes, including brushes, cloths or pads that may also incorporate compartments or applicators for shoe polish. [A47L 23/10] Cloth or collapsible cleaning pads -- A47L 23/10 pertains to cloth or collapsible cleaning pads employed for cleaning footwear, fitting within the Human Necessities group under Furniture and the Domestic washing or cleaning subclass. [A47L 23/12] Cleaning devices for suede -- A47L 23/12 covers devices specifically designed for cleaning suede footwear, including brushes, rollers, sponges or other apparatuses that gently remove dirt without damaging the nap. [A47L 23/14] Implements for use during shoe-cleaning, to guard against spoiling -- This subclass covers devices used while cleaning shoes that prevent the footwear or surrounding surfaces from becoming soiled or damaged, such as protective mats, shoe-covers, trays or guarded brushes.
[A47L 23/16] Shoe-shine stands -- A47L 23/16 covers shoe-shine stands, devices designed to hold footwear securely while it is being polished or cleaned.
